author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/test/0/test_90
body:
What is it, one wonders, that compels intelligent, highly qualified women to choose, of their own free will, to work in the City of London? The salaries, obviously. But even so. For the environment, as we now know from a parade of discrimination and unfair-dismissal claims, is brutish, sexist, and deeply unpleasant — though it is worth noting, in fairness, that it is not as bad as the police force, a fast-food chain, a men's magazine, a mini-brothel, or the Church of England Synod. Without these claims, indeed, the City might still be posing as a model equal-opportunities employer, its reputation for decency intact, its women smiling through their teeth in the interests of a six-figure bonus.

The lineage of City complainants begins, arguably, with Nicola Horlick, who in 1997 transformed what appears to have been a grubby staff-poaching dispute into a moral crusade, dragging entranced reporters with her to Frankfurt and back, as if the liberation of oppressed fund managers were a cause comparable to the campaign against apartheid. Her recovery was, as these things go, remarkably swift. She was soon back in business, managing money, being photographed, and giving interviews about the superwoman's capacity to have it all. Her successors, however, have demanded ever-larger sums to compensate for bullying, spitting, name-calling — one was called "hot totty" — and incidents involving lapdancing. The figures have risen, as if in some grotesque inflationary spiral, from the modest to the astronomical.

Consider Claire Bright, who is seeking £11m from HBOS, where she earned £600,000, having allegedly suffered under a man she described as "a mini Hitler". One hesitates to dwell on the comparison, but given the size of a City office versus the size of Nazi Germany, only a mini-Holocaust was ever achievable. The phrase, one supposes, was not intended to be taken literally, but it does rather invite the question of what a full-sized Hitler might accomplish in a trading floor, and whether the compensation would then be commensurately enlarged.

Then there are the six Dresdner Kleinwort Wasserstein bankers who collectively demanded £800m — £800m! — for offences including being called "the Pamela Anderson of trading" and witnessing their CEO date his assistant. One does not wish to minimise the distress of being likened to a Canadian actress, or of watching one's boss conduct a romance with a subordinate. But £800m is a sum that, in other contexts, might rebuild a small country. It is the kind of money that makes you wonder whether the claimants have fully grasped the distinction between a workplace grievance and the Treaty of Versailles.

To put these figures in perspective, one might consider the payouts from the Criminal Injuries Compensation Board for victims of the July 7 bombings. Those who lost limbs received roughly £50,000 per leg. A leg, apparently, is worth £50,000. A Pamela Anderson comparison, by contrast, appears to be worth somewhere in the region of £133m, assuming the £800m is split six ways. One does not wish to be crude about these things, but the contrast is so stark, so grotesquely illuminating, that it hardly requires editorialising. The bombs on the Underground were not, as far as one is aware, a workplace dispute. The victims did not have access to employment tribunals. They were simply on their way to work, or going about their business, when they were blown up. Their compensation, such as it was, bore no relation whatsoever to the sums now being demanded by people who, however badly treated, were not, in the end, physically dismembered.

One begins to wonder whether newspapers should even be advertising these gargantuan claims, since they bear almost no relation to real-world settlements — most employment tribunals award sums in the tens of thousands, not the hundreds of millions — and may, by their very prominence, inflict further pain on the still-traumatised victims of actual violence, and indeed on their lawyers, who must contemplate the gap between a leg and a lapdance with a sinking feeling.

**Can schools be protected from forces of darkness?**

Meanwhile, in the Guardian this week, an advertisement has been placed by Oasis UK, the charity of the Rev Steve Chalke, seeking an "accomplished fundraiser" to realise a vision of "outstanding schools and community hubs" rooted in Christian values. The successful candidate will, the ad explains, be a "high value dealer" — or HVD, in the jargon of the trade — which is not, as one might initially suppose, a term from The Wire, but a description of someone who can extract large sums from wealthy people in exchange for the warm feeling that accompanies having one's name attached to a school. Neither the fundraiser, nor indeed Rev Chalke himself, appears to need any actual experience of running a school. This is, presumably, considered a minor detail, rather like a hospital advertising for a chief executive who can raise money but has never seen a patient.

The broader picture is even more curious. The government has, it seems, decided that children's education should depend on amateurs — homophobic religious fanatics, self-promoting tradesmen, would-be barons, parents, classroom assistants — anyone, in fact, except people who have actually trained to teach. Peter Hyman, Blair's former speechwriter, went to Islington Green school, announcing that he wanted "to do something different", which is one way of describing the transition from writing the prime minister's speeches to writing on a blackboard. Reg Vardy, the creationist school sponsor, has been permitted to attach his name to institutions where children will presumably learn that the Earth was created in six days, and that fossils were placed in rocks by a deity with a mischievous sense of humour. One does not need to be a militant secularist to wonder whether children's minds will be safe in such hands, or whether the forces of darkness might not, in this context, be more accurately described as the forces of well-meaning incompetence.

**This week**

I have been reading Naomi Wolf's *The Treehouse*, which has prompted in me a powerful desire to revisit Laura Ingalls Wilder's *Little House in the Big Woods*, in which Pa demonstrates far superior life lessons, not to mention more practical instruction in cabin-building, than anything Wolf's father appears to have offered from the confines of his arboreal retreat. Pa, at least, knew how to butcher a pig. I have also, shamefully, been enjoying George Galloway's self-destruction on *Celebrity Big Brother*, which has provided more entertainment than anything available in the City of London, at any price.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_2
body:
It is a curious feature of magazine publishing that by the time a glossy profile reaches the newsstands, the subject has often moved on — not, in the case of politicians, to higher things, but into the ditch. So it is with this week's offering from *Tatler*, or possibly *GQ*, or some other publication whose art department favours moody monochrome and whose editors believe that a politician photographed from below, jaw thrust forward, eyes narrowed against an unseen wind, is a politician reborn. The piece, filed six weeks ago, presents its subject as a figure of extraordinary resilience, a man who has, in the words of the interviewer, "reinvented himself before and will do so again." There is talk of morning runs, of a new diet, of the restorative company of his children. A colleague is quoted describing him as "forensic" in his analysis, "relentless" in his pursuit of the Opposition, "incisive" in committee. Another calls him "the most intellectually formidable leader since..." — and here the trailing ellipsis does a great deal of work, since the identity of the predecessor is, at the time of going to press, too embarrassing to specify.

By the time the magazine hits the coffee tables, the leader in question has lost two by-elections, been photographed looking stricken outside a village hall in a constituency the party held for forty years, and become the subject of a sketch in the *Daily Telegraph* in which he is compared, unfavourably, to a man trying to sell a second-hand car that has already been written off twice. The "forensic" quality that so impressed the interviewer now looks, in the unforgiving light of consecutive electoral disasters, less like analytical brilliance than like the cold pedantry of someone who can tell you exactly why the house is burning down while standing in the flames. The "relentlessness" has curdled into the tunnel vision of a man who cannot read a room. The "incisiveness" — well, the less said about incisiveness the better, since the last incision removed what little support remained in the marginals.

This is the familiar arc. One thinks of his two immediate predecessors, each of whom was, in turn, hailed as the saviour of the party, each of whom was photographed for a magazine in a posture of confident renewal, and each of whom was, within eighteen months, discarded like a washing machine past its warranty. The first had been praised for his "intellectual heft" and "common touch" — an improbable combination that turned out to be neither intellectual nor common — before being removed in a putsch so bloodless that the sketch writers compared it to a mercy killing. The second was lauded for his "authenticity," a quality which, on closer inspection, turned out to consist largely of not wearing a tie and saying "folks" a lot. He too was summarily replaced, his departure so swift that his resignation letter was, according to one reporter, still warm when his successor's acceptance speech began.

The pattern is now so well established that one might reasonably compare the party's leadership turnover to the life cycle of a budget washing machine. There is the initial excitement of installation — the new cycles, the quiet motor, the impressive spin speed. There is the brief honeymoon period during which everything comes out fresh. Then, gradually, the drum begins to make a disquieting noise. The machine is repaired once, repaired again. Eventually the warranty expires, the repairman shrugs, and the thing is hauled to the tip, to be replaced by an identical model from the same factory, which will perform identically and break down on the same schedule. The party, one gathers, is already shopping for the next one. This model will, if the trend continues, have an even shorter functional life than its predecessor — perhaps nine months, perhaps six — before the same grinding noise begins and the same men in high-vis jackets arrive to take it away.

The environmental consequences of this continual disposal have not, as far as one can tell, been considered by anyone. The accumulating waste of discarded leaders — each one a substantial unit of ambition, briefing documents, and lightly used media training — must surely be taking its toll on the local authority's landfill capacity. Perhaps the party could arrange for them to be recycled, the more durable components extracted and repurposed: one former leader's gift for bland reassurance could be melted down for use in a press release; another's talent for factional intrigue might be converted into compost. But for now, they simply pile up, rusting gently in the political equivalent of a council amenity site, alongside broken tumble dryers and the abandoned policy launches of governments past.

**How the harsh house system built the left**

It has been noted, in the obituaries of a prominent left-wing journalist who died last week, that he was educated at a minor public school of the sort that specialises in producing men who spend the rest of their lives atoning for the privilege. The house system, with its ritual humiliations, its enforced loyalties, its fagging and its cold baths, appears to have been the forge in which his anti-establishment convictions were tempered. One reads these obituaries and reflects that the British left, as a political force, owes its existence almost entirely to the cruelty of the house system. Remove the house system and you remove the grievance that powered a century of dissent. It follows, does it not, that the current fashion for softening these schools — for replacing fagging with pastoral care, and cold baths with counselling — poses a genuine threat to the future of revolutionary sentiment. Where, one wonders, will the next generation of angry class warriors come from, if not from the dormitories of institutions that taught them, through systematic petty tyranny, to despise authority? One thinks of the contemporary musician whose entire anti-establishment aesthetic was, by his own account, forged in the moderately strict environment of a suburban grammar school, where the house points system and the competitive cross-country runs instilled in him a lifelong suspicion of organised competition. The irony, then, is exquisite: a government minister's earnest promotion of competitive school sport may, through this logic, end up producing more revolutionaries for the far left than any Socialist Workers Party recruitment drive could ever hope to generate.

**A lottery of friends**

A report this week informs us that adults in Britain have too few interracial friendships, and ought to do something about it. In a spirit of self-examination, I have tallied my own social circle and find it grievously lacking — not merely in racial diversity, but in every conceivable category of person. I have no Welsh friends. I have no friends who are estate agents, though this is perhaps deliberate. I have no baronet friends, no vegan friends, no friends who play the viola, no friends who keep alpacas, no friends who have appeared on *Question Time*, and no friends who would admit to having appeared on *Question Time* if they had. The report does not explain how adults are supposed to acquire the mandated diversity of friendships. Are we to apply for friendship leave from our employers, during which we are assigned a carefully curated cohort of new acquaintances, each representing a different demographic category? Or is there to be a lottery system, in which citizens are randomly assigned twenty new friends, sight unseen, and required to maintain the relationships under penalty of a small fine? If so, one can only pity the unlucky citizen who opens her letter from the Friendship Diversity Board and discovers that she has been assigned, among others, a man who collects model railways, a woman who sells timeshares in Marbella, and a heron.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_5
body:
It is now some years since Susie Orbach, the therapist who first introduced us to the proposition that fat is a feminist issue, launched her "Antidote" campaign to bring "emotional literacy" into British public life. Among the signatories was Tessa Jowell, then a minister of some kind, who presumably appreciated the value of being in touch with one's feelings while simultaneously not being in touch with the details of one's husband's finances. At the time, the campaign struck one as a worthy, if slightly ambitious, exercise — the sort of thing that might result in a few more workplace counsellors and a correspondingly smaller number of boardroom tantrums — but unlikely to transform the emotional vocabulary of Westminster, where the dominant feelings have always been ambition, resentment, and the desire to appear on television.

How wrong one was. The campaign has, in its way, succeeded beyond anything Orbach could have imagined. Not in the sense that politicians have become more emotionally honest — that would require a revolution in character rather than vocabulary — but in the sense that they have acquired, and now deploy with surgical precision, the language of the marriage counsellor. When the prime minister receives an apology from someone or other — a colleague, an adviser, a newspaper — he announces that it allows everyone to "draw a line and move on." His press secretary, asked an unwelcome question about the propriety of some arrangement or other, says the matter is now closed and that it is time to "move on." The culture secretary, confronted with questions about an appointment process that smells faintly of yesterday's fish, says she is satisfied that the right procedures were followed, that there is "closure" on the issue, and that it is time, once again, for everyone to "move on." Coordinated, these utterances sound less like the responses of elected politicians to legitimate scrutiny and more like the facilitator's script at a group therapy session for the nation, in which the patients are not invited to speak.

The therapeutic vocabulary has, in fact, become a weapon of considerable utility. When a politician says "we need to draw a line and move on," what he means is not that a painful episode has been processed and resolved through honest confrontation, but something closer to: "I've won, so bugger off." When he talks of "closure," he does not mean the arduous psychological work of coming to terms with a loss, but the political convenience of no longer having to answer questions about it. The language of healing has been repurposed as a means of shutting things down.

Were I to lie on the prime minister's couch — and one imagines the couch would be leather, probably beige, positioned near a window overlooking a garden where important decisions about decking are made — I would describe feeling, as the therapists say, "stuck." I feel stuck because I was promised things. I was promised, variously, that there would be no increase in tuition fees, that the NHS was safe in government hands, that there would be no compromise on standards in public appointments, and that the country would not be taken into a war on the basis of evidence that turned out to be a tissue of conjectures assembled by people who thought the word "dodgy" constituted a professional assessment. I feel, if I am honest, betrayed. I feel unable to trust. I feel that the person who hurt me is, as it happens, getting on with his life — launching his next inquiry, announcing his next initiative, appearing on television with that expression of earnest concern which suggests that he, too, has been through a great deal and deserves credit for surviving it.

For guidance in these difficult times, I would recommend, to anyone feeling similarly stuck, a slim volume called *Forgive and Be Free: A Seven-Step Journey to Moving On*, by Dr Miriam Gottlieb, a therapist whose previous works include *The Healing Power of Letting Go* and *Why Your Anger Is Really About Your Mother*. Dr Gottlieb's framework, while not specifically designed for citizens of democracies whose leaders have misled them, is surprisingly applicable. In Step Three, she advises the wounded party to recall the "good times" with the person who hurt them. "Think back," she writes, "to the moments of joy, the shared laughter, the sense that you were both on the same side." Very well. The good times: the time the prime minister said education was his top priority. The time the home secretary said she would be tough on crime and tough on the causes of crime. The time the chancellor said there would be no return to boom and bust. The time the culture secretary said the BBC chairmanship would be filled through an open, transparent, and rigorous process. These were, in their way, beautiful moments, and recalling them does produce a powerful emotion, though perhaps not the one Dr Gottlieb intends.

In Step Five, Dr Gottlieb observes that "the person who hurt you is getting on with their life, and you must get on with yours." This is, one has to concede, accurate. The prime minister is indeed getting on with his life. He has already moved on to the next inquiry, the next relaunch, the next expression of sincere concern about something he has just made worse. The question is why politicians, who have never previously shown much interest in the inner lives of others — unless those others were focus-grouped — should have become so fluent in the language of counselling. Real therapists, as opposed to politicians impersonating them, tend to define "moving on" and "closure" as laborious, often painful processes, requiring honesty, self-examination, and a willingness to sit with discomfort rather than simply declaring, by fiat, that the discomfort is over. "Closure," in particular, is the most abject of counselling clichés — a word so overused by people who want to stop talking about something that it now means approximately nothing — but even in its degraded state, it refers to something one achieves for oneself. You cannot, as any therapist worth their hourly rate will tell you, claim closure on someone else's behalf. You cannot march into a patient's session and announce that, as far as you are concerned, the matter is now closed, and they should be moving on. Though this, of course, is precisely what the prime minister does.

**The guarantor of fairness and his many, many roles**

For anyone wishing to understand how the government's therapeutic vocabulary operates in practice, the recent appointment of a new BBC chairman provides an instructive case study. The process, which was supposed to be open, transparent, and rigorous — those words again — has been defended by the culture secretary with a devotion and a consistency that suggests she has been attending daily sessions. At every opportunity, she invokes the name of the man she appointed to oversee the selection process, a person of such surpassing qualifications that the mere mention of his name is meant to cause all questions about the appointment's integrity to dissolve, like a grievance in the presence of a competent therapist.

Who is this guarantor of fairness? He is, we are told, the answer to every question about the process. Was it proper? Ask him. Was it transparent? He oversaw it. Was the best candidate chosen? He was in charge. Who oversees the overseer? This, the culture secretary explains, is not a question she proposes to answer, and indeed, one senses that she considers it a rather aggressive line of inquiry, the sort of thing a person who was unable to "move on" would ask.

The overseer himself is a figure of remarkable profile. His CV, which runs to several pages of small type, lists, among other distinctions: a professorship at one institution, an honorary professorship at another, a visiting professorship at a third, a fellowship at a fourth, a non-executive directorship of a company that consults on leadership, a chairmanship of a board that advises on governance, a seat on the council of an organisation dedicated to excellence, a role as senior adviser to a firm whose name suggests dynamism, and a salary from a public body that he also, in a different capacity, advises on matters of public appointments. He is, in short, the kind of person whose business card requires a fold.

His leadership philosophy, set out in a book he has written and several he has contributed to, is equally impressive. He believes in "flying at 30,000 feet" — a metaphor drawn, one assumes, from the experience of someone who has not been in economy class for a very long time — while also knowing when to "land and walk." He has compiled a list of "unspoken employee requests," which include "acknowledge the greatness within me" and "help me see my role in the bigger picture." He has written a poem about life as a circus, in which the reader is invited to consider whether they are "the acrobat, the ringmaster, or the clown." And he has adapted the leadership principles of General George Patton, whose maxims include the memorable instruction not to "let adverse facts stand in the way of a good decision."

Whether this last principle informed the BBC chairmanship appointment is not a matter on which the culture secretary would presume to comment. Judging the overseer's performance, she would remind us, is "entirely a matter" for the overseer. And anyone who thinks otherwise probably needs to draw a line and move on.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_6
body:
"I know that the cynics will sneer," the prime minister warned, last week, as he unveiled his latest exercise in mass consultation, the Big Dialogue. "They'll say it's a stunt, a charade, a PR exercise. But I believe the British people are ready for a genuine, grown-up conversation about the future of this country." He was, as it happens, quite right about the cynics. They will sneer. They are sneering already. But then, the prime minister has a gift for issuing predictions that confirm themselves even as they leave his mouth, rather in the manner of a man who, having set fire to the curtains, warns that the house may shortly be engulfed in flames.

The Big Dialogue, we are told, is different from all previous national conversations, listening exercises, policy reviews, and people's panels, in that this time the government really means it. A website has been launched. Citizens are urged to contribute. "Nothing is off limits," the prime minister's spokesman promised, in a phrase that, on past form, means that everything is. Already, the site is open for business, and the citizenry have been filing in, like visitors to a model village, to admire the little arrangements that have been made for them.

What is striking, on even the most cursory inspection of the published contributions, is the tone. If this is a conversation, it is one conducted entirely in the language of a testimonial dinner. "I would like to thank the government for the unprecedented prosperity we now enjoy," writes M. Henderson of Solihull. "Under the previous administration, my family lived in a state of perpetual anxiety. Now we feel secure, optimistic, and proud to be British." A Mrs P. Whitmore of Exeter is similarly transported: "For the first time in my life, I feel that the government is actually listening to ordinary people. The contrast with the misery and neglect of the Tory years could not be starker." D. Akhtar of Bradford contributes a paean to the "transformation" of his local schools, before concluding, "I only wish the government knew how grateful we are."

One could go on. In fact, one rather wishes one could stop. The contributions are so uniformly fawning, so suspiciously alike in their structure — gratitude, contrast with the bad old days, a constructive suggestion so mild as to be invisible — that one begins to wonder whether they have been composed by the same hand, or at any rate by the same focus group. Even the constructive criticism has the quality of a love letter that briefly mentions the recipient's haircut: "If I have one small criticism," writes T. Nichols of Guildford, "it is that the government could perhaps communicate its achievements even more effectively." Another contributor, K. Renshaw of Carlisle, wonders whether "more could be done to celebrate the success of existing initiatives." So it goes on, an endless, contented murmur, like the sound of a microwave humming in an empty kitchen.

Among the offerings, one contribution stands out for its vivid, if unacknowledged, literary quality. "Before the Revolution," it begins, "London was a gloomy place, with narrow streets and crumbling buildings, where the people lived in a state of perpetual want and fear. The capitalists owned everything and the workers owned nothing. The streets were filthy, the children went hungry, and there was no freedom." It is, as any reader of Orwell will recognise, a fair summary of the party history textbook passage from *1984*, that fictional primer in the arts of official nostalgia, in which the pre-Revolution world is depicted as an unrelieved nightmare so that the present, however grim, may seem a deliverance. Whether the contributor is a satirist, a plagiarist, or simply someone who has not read the book and has arrived independently at the same register, is impossible to determine. What is certain is that the passage has been published, without comment, alongside the tributes from Solihull and Exeter, as if it were merely another expression of public contentment. Which, in its way, it is.

What is conspicuously absent from the Big Dialogue is any reference to the subjects that might reasonably be described as the major political issues of the day. There is no mention of the war. No one, among the thousands of grateful contributors, appears to have raised the small matter of the tens of thousands of British troops deployed in a conflict whose justification has shifted, over the years, with the fluency of a man explaining why he was found in a hotel room he had no business occupying. Terrorism, that perennial preoccupation of the state, does not feature. Constitutional reform, the future of the Lords, the state of public services, the creeping authoritarianism of a government that has introduced more criminal offences than any of its predecessors — none of these has made it through whatever filtering process separates the raw contributions from the ones deemed suitable for public display.

What does get through is either hopelessly parochial — a petition for a zebra crossing in Woking, a plea for the restoration of a village bus service — or carefully redirected toward the citizenry themselves. "We must all take more responsibility for our own health," writes one contributor, in a formulation that could have been dictated by any health minister in the past decade. "The problem is not government policy but the attitudes of ordinary people," offers another, with the touching faith of a prisoner who has been told that his cell is, in fact, a studio flat. The complaints that survive publication, in other words, are either so small as to be harmless, or so obligingly self-flagellating as to require no response from the state. The Big Dialogue, it turns out, is a conversation in which one party does all the listening and the other party does all the apologising — but only for things that are already, conveniently, its own fault.

The whole exercise bears an uncanny resemblance to Stalin's Potemkin villages, those portable facades of prosperous hamlets assembled for the edification of visiting dignitaries, behind which the actual villages lay, in their unimproved state, with their unimproved inhabitants. Except that the Big Dialogue is more efficient, since the visitors and the villagers are the same people. It also recalls nothing so much as a pre-1989 edition of *Pravda*, in which the letters page was invariably filled with the grateful observations of workers who had been miraculously transformed, by the act of writing to the newspaper, into amateur propagandists. "Comrade Editor," they would begin, "I would like to express my profound gratitude for the excellent supply of tractor parts at Factory 47." The Big Dialogue's contributors are not, of course, required to address anyone as "Comrade," but the spirit is not so very different.

Whether this uniformity of tone reflects active censorship — a diligent hand deleting every contribution that mentions Iraq, or civil liberties, or the prime minister's credibility — or mere public apathy, is a question that admits of no easy answer. It is possible that the British public, having been invited to so many national conversations, has simply run out of things to say, and that the grateful contributors of Solihull and Exeter are the last citizens in the country who still have the energy to fill in a form. It is equally possible that the contributions selected for publication are as representative of the total submissions as a hotel brochure is representative of the actual guest experience. In the absence of any published analysis, summary, or even a polite "thank you" from the prime minister — who, on past form, will acknowledge the public's contributions roughly when he acknowledges his own mistakes — we are left to draw our own conclusions.

What is certain is that the initiative's name, the Big Dialogue, with its echoes of Orwell's Big Brother, was perhaps not the most carefully considered piece of branding. Then again, perhaps it was. After all, in *1984*, the regime does not suppress public opinion so much as manufacture it, replacing genuine dissent with a synthetic consensus so seamless that no one, not even the synthesised, can tell the difference. The cynics, as the prime minister predicted, are indeed sneering. But then, when a government launches a national conversation and calls it the Big Dialogue, the cynics are not so much sneering as reading the instructions.

**Return to sender**

No sooner had the government invited us to talk to it than another public intellectual announced that he wished to return his MBE. The gesture, which has become a minor ritual of the conscience-stricken commentariat, was accompanied by the familiar explanation: honours, the intellectual explained, are tarnished by their association with an establishment he can no longer, in conscience, endorse. He conceded, with the magnanimity that comes naturally to those who have newspaper columns, that "the little people" — nurses, dinner ladies, community workers — might still find such recognition meaningful. "For them," he wrote, "it is a genuine honour. But for those of us with a platform, it would be hypocritical to accept." One is reminded of Leona Helmsley's observation that "only little people pay taxes," though the intellectual's version is, if anything, more striking in its condescension: only little people, it seems, should be grateful.

Benjamin Zephaniah, who famously refused an honour on the grounds that the word "empire" still clung to it like a bad smell, at least had the excuse that the honour's name was, in itself, an affront. The latest refusenik has no such linguistic objection. He simply wishes us to know that he is too important to accept a bauble that, by his own admission, would mean a great deal to someone less important than himself. One pictures a municipal infrastructure for the disposal of unwanted honours: a recycling scheme, perhaps, with brightly coloured bins outside town halls, marked "MBE," "OBE," "CBE," in which conscience-stricken columnists may deposit their medals, to be cleaned, repacketed, and redistributed to dinner ladies who have not, as yet, secured a weekly slot in a national newspaper.

A simpler solution, perhaps, would be for future honours lists to be accompanied by a supplementary "returned honours" list, published alongside the conventional one, so that conscience-stricken ordinary people — lollipop ladies, hospital porters, community gardeners — might briefly experience the feeling of being important enough to refuse something. It is, after all, the same feeling that intellectual columnists already enjoy every week, through the simple expedient of having a newspaper print their names.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/validation/0/validation_2
body:
Is the glass half-full, or is it half-empty? This, as the prime minister never tires of reminding us, is the question that separates the constructive from the carping, the forward-looking from the perpetually dissatisfied. Never mind that both options describe the same glass, containing the same unappetising liquid. Never mind that the choice is, as it happens, between two outcomes neither of which any sane person would drink. On one side of this particular tumbler sits a self-branding mogul whose capacity for public emotional display rivals, in its darkly comic intensity, anything Dickens conceived for the death of Little Nell — the quivering lip, the wounded eyes, the sense of a great man brought low by the ingratitude of lesser mortals. On the other, a corporate operator so widely despised that its annual profits are routinely cited as evidence of moral collapse, a company that treats public obligations the way a cat treats a litter tray. And you, prime minister, must choose. Or rather, you must pretend to choose, while ensuring that nothing of consequence changes at all.

It was not always thus. Or rather, it was always thus, but it was not always admitted. In opposition, your party was magnificent on this subject. Righteous, thundering, practically Old Testament. Your front bench could not find enough ways to denounce the profit-seeking operator and its grubby monopoly. The then shadow minister — now, by one of those coincidences that make democratic politics such a relentless entertainment — told anyone who would listen that the current arrangement was "a scandal" and "an outrage against the people." He had, he said, studied the models operating in other European countries, where non-profit structures delivered superior service without the indignity of shareholder dividends. The leader of the house, then a humble shadow transport spokesman, promised "a new deal for the public," in which "the people's interests, not private profit," would guide the licensing system. You yourself, prime minister, in a speech whose sincerity I would not dream of questioning, pledged to replace the existing operator with "a model that serves the many, not the few." The many nodded. The few, one assumed, trembled.

The Dome, that other great monument to governmental ambition untempered by competence, springs inevitably to mind. You will remember the Dome. Everyone else has tried to forget it, but it persists, like a migraine. The parallels are, by now, almost too obvious to enumerate: a grand project announced with fanfare, entrusted to people whose principal qualification was proximity to power, and destined to end in recrimination, judicial intervention, and the quiet realisation that no one of political consequence would be held accountable. But in one respect the licensing saga is, if anything, worse than the Dome. The Dome was, at least, the government's own folly — conceived in hubris, executed in confusion, but genuinely its own. The licensing fiasco is worse because the government actually opposed the corporate operator when it sat on the other side of the house. It campaigned against it. It denounced it. It promised to replace it. And then, in office, it discovered that the operator was rather more difficult to dislodge than a few sturdy sentences in a conference speech had suggested. The shared feature, however, remains: both failures are now being framed as forces of nature — vast, complex, uncontrollable systems that no human could reasonably be expected to manage. As if the licensing system were a hurricane, and the commission merely a startled bystander.

Which brings us to the commission itself. One might reasonably ask why the responsible minister — a person whose name I shall charitably spare, for the moment — appointed a body so conspicuously lacking in relevant expertise that one half-expected its members to be selected by lottery, or by the ancient method of examining the entrails of sacrificial birds. The chair, in particular, remains a mystery of appointment that future historians may wish to examine alongside other great imponderables, such as the selection of Jeffrey Archer as deputy chairman of the Conservative party. What qualifications did this person possess? What relevant experience? The answers, so far as anyone has been able to determine, are: none, and none. But the chair was, we are assured, a person of "considerable standing," which is the sort of thing people say when they cannot think of anything else.

Presented with only two bids — two — the commission managed to dismiss both, but then, in a move that would have most toddlers reeling with sympathetic indignation, gave one bidder a second chance. Guess which one. Yes. The pompous one. The one with the self-branding and the quivering lip and the friends in the right places. The other bidder, the despised corporate operator, was sent away, like a naughty child, to think about what it had done. That this piece of favouritism was subsequently deemed, by a judge, to be "conspicuously unfair," "unlawful," and "an abuse of power" — language more commonly associated with tinpot dictatorships than with the deliberations of a British licensing commission — gives some indication of the scale of the problem.

The spurned operator, being a corporate entity of the sort that keeps lawyers in the manner to which they have become accustomed, sought and obtained judicial review. The judge's language, as noted, was withering. One might have expected the chair to resign immediately, or at least to have the decency to look embarrassed in public. Instead, the chair was initially supported by the minister, in a display of institutional solidarity so touching it almost restored one's faith in human loyalty. Almost. Eventually, the chair departed — not, be it noted, because of the unlawful decision, or the abuse of power, or the conspicuous unfairness, but because of "media vilification," which is the modern equivalent of being driven out by a torch-wielding mob. The remaining commissioners, meanwhile, have decided to stay on under a new, successor chair, who acknowledges that they are discredited but insists there is no time to replace them. No time. One might have thought that the passage of several months, during which the entire process was judicially overturned and restarted, constituted rather a lot of time. But one is not a commissioner.

The losing bidder, it should be said, has form. It has a history of litigation that suggests a corporate culture in which the courtroom is regarded as a first, rather than a last, resort. And so, in the absence of competent political leadership, the entire saga has been effectively settled by lawyers. The treasury solicitors approved the initial, unfair decision. A corporate QC, retained at considerable expense, overturned it. And now a City firm is overseeing the final deliberations — a firm which has, by a coincidence so remarkable it would strain the credulity of a Victorian novelist, previously worked for all the interested parties. One imagines the partners drawing straws to determine which conflict of interest to declare first.

Given the choice between the incompetent political appointees and the brainy lawyers, the conclusion is obvious. Why not simply abandon the pretense of democratic government and let the lawyers run everything? The treasury solicitors can draft the legislation, the QCs can execute it, and the City firms can adjudicate any disputes, billing by the hour, as is their ancient custom. No more commissions. No more consultations. No more ministers pretending to make decisions they have already delegated to someone who charges £400 an hour. And the beauty of it, prime minister, is that you are already doing exactly that. You have been appointing lords and lawyers to govern for years. You simply haven't finished yet.

---

**Related links**

- "A model that serves the many" — full text of the prime minister's 2003 conference speech, in which the licensing promise was made [pdf]
- Judicial review ruling: R (Despised Operator Ltd) v Licensing Commission [2005] EWHC — includes the judge's full remarks on "conspicuous unfairness"
- The Dome: a retrospective — Guardian interactive timeline, "What was it for? We still don't know"
- Statement from the new chair: "I acknowledge the commission is discredited. There is no time to replace it. Please stop asking."
- Professor Lord Robert Winston on the benefits of Omega-3 milk: "It's clever milk" — unrelated, but instructive

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_103
body:
While the British media has been obsessing about the Home Secretary's love affair, the deadliest conflict since the second world war has resumed. The Rwandan army has crossed back into the north-eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). Rival factions of the Congolese army, some of them loyal to Rwanda, are fighting each other. Civilians are being killed, raped, tortured and forced to flee into the forests. The International Rescue Committee has found that even before the latest fighting, over 1,000 people were dying every day from disease and malnutrition caused by the previous conflict. Nearly half of them were children under five. The total death toll now stands at some 3.8 million. And hardly anyone has noticed.

This is not altogether surprising. The conflict in the DRC is complicated, and the press has little patience with complexity. But there is another reason for our silence, which tells us something far more disturbing about the way we view the world. We cannot accept that the victims of one of the worst crimes in history have become some of the world's worst perpetrators.

Rwanda's initial invasions of the DRC, then still called Zaire, possessed an apparent justification. The Interahamwe militias who killed 800,000 Rwandans in the 1994 genocide fled across the border, where they were sheltered first by President Mobutu, then by Laurent Kabila, and from where they threatened to reinvade Rwanda. Paul Kagame's Rwandan Patriotic Front had every reason to pursue them. But after the second invasion in 1998, something changed. Rwanda abandoned the pursuit of the genocidaires in favour of the pursuit of minerals. By 1999, according to a report by the UN Security Council's panel of experts, 80% of Rwanda's military budget — some $320 million a year — was being financed by stolen Congolese diamonds and coltan, the ore from which tantalum, the metal used in mobile phones, is extracted. The pursuit of justice had become the business of theft.

The scale of the catastrophe this unleashed beggars comprehension. Six foreign armies were drawn into what became a vast African turf war over the DRC's mineral wealth. Millions of people were displaced. Thousands were forced into mining or prostitution. Rwanda's operation, according to Amnesty International, was the most efficient: controlled directly from Kigali rather than left to the discretion of commanders in the field. It continued even after the official withdrawal of Rwandan troops in 2002. The latest invasion looks like a thinly disguised effort to neutralise the militias threatening Rwanda's mineral business.

Why, then, has the West not responded? We wring our hands over Darfur, and rightly so. But we appear to be indifferent to Congo, where the death toll is some 60 times higher. The explanation, I believe, is that the public is accustomed to seeing Rwandan forces as the good guys — genocide survivors who drove out the killers. It is psychologically difficult to accept that victims can become perpetrators, and some of the bloodiest war criminals on earth.

There is a counterargument, which has been deployed by some of Kagame's more tenacious defenders. It suggests that the criticism of his government is driven by power plays on the UN Security Council, and in particular by France's determination to deflect attention from its own complicity in the 1994 genocide. It is true that France has its own sordid agenda. But it is impossible to dismiss the findings of the UN on these grounds when the same conclusions have been reached by Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, the International Crisis Group, Global Witness, the British all-party parliamentary group on the Great Lakes region, and the US State Department. These are not organisations which can be casually written off as tools of French foreign policy.

The reports these bodies have published run to hundreds of pages. They contain eyewitness accounts, testimony from survivors and from perpetrators. They document the rape of children as young as three. They describe the genital mutilation of women who tried to resist. They record forced labour in the mines, with victims buried alive when shafts collapsed. They detail the torching of villages, the looting of crops and cattle, the killing of resisters, and the forcing of millions of people into the jungle, where they died of starvation and disease. Most of the 3.8 million deaths were caused by malnutrition and disease, but the marauding armies which displaced the population bear equivalent responsibility. The documentation is specific: it names agents and victims, dates and locations, the value of stolen resources and the names of the companies which bought them.

Some of Kagame's defenders argue that critics confuse Rwanda's disciplined army with the chaotic rebel groups operating in the eastern Congo. Victoria Brittain, for example, has suggested that the atrocities are the work of militias, not of regular Rwandan troops. But the US State Department's summary of "numerous credible reports" tells a different story. It documents regular Rwandan troops killing, torturing and raping in the provinces of Kivu and Maniema. In any case, there is no moral difference between killing people directly and commissioning proxies to do so. The RCD-Goma militia, which has been responsible for some of the worst atrocities in the eastern Congo, was formed in Kigali, financed and equipped by Rwanda, and supplied with heavy weapons by the Rwandan army, according to Global Witness and Amnesty International. It is a proxy force, created and sustained by Rwanda, and its crimes are Rwanda's crimes.

The other excuse Rwanda offers is that it is pursuing the Interahamwe. It is true that the government in Kinshasa has also committed crimes, and that it has armed and supported some of the militias fighting Rwanda's proxies. But Kinshasa's authority barely runs in the east of the country. A UN report leaked to the BBC confirms that Rwanda and its proxies remain the dominant forces in the region, controlling most of the minerals and involved in nearly all the fighting. The Interahamwe, moreover, are now much weaker than they were in 1994. Rwanda could have destroyed them years ago. The International Crisis Group has pointed out that Rwanda had total military control of eastern Congo from 1996 to 2002, and failed to neutralise them. The British parliamentary group found that Rwandan forces were often stationed where no Interahamwe existed, and that they sometimes even allied with them to control the mines. Rwanda is using the old excuse to drag eastern Congo back into war.

The international community could put a stop to this. Rwanda is a tiny, fragile state, dependent on foreign aid for half its budget. Over a third of that aid comes from Britain. We have the leverage, if we had the will. But nothing will change until the public wakes up to this war, and stops pretending that the victims of atrocious crimes cannot also be perpetrators.

www.monbiot.com

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_104
body:
It is becoming the received wisdom of the oppositional left: George Bush's America is converging upon fascism. Barbara Probst Solomon and Richard Sennett have both argued the case in print, and the internet hums with the same contention, expressed with varying degrees of sophistication. Sometimes the fascism is described as "soft"; sometimes it is presented as the full-blown variety, merely biding its time. Either way, the diagnosis is the same, and either way, it is wrong.

Robert Paxton, in The Anatomy of Fascism, defines the phenomenon as a politics of obsession with community decline, humiliation, or victimhood, compensatory cults of unity, energy, and purity, and mass-based nationalist commitment. Fascism, crucially, was allied to elite interests but it arose from below: it was produced by hysterical popular mobilisation. Whatever else you might say about Bush's America, it does not mobilise. It demobilises. It cultivates fear, passivity, and deference. Its rallies are stage-managed; its crowds are screened; its street violence is subcontracted to the state. There are no blackshirts in the squares. There is no mass party. There is, instead, a sullen, frightened compliance, which is a very different thing. To call this fascism is not merely to misread the present; it is to ensure that we fail to recognise what is actually happening. For Bush's project is not unprecedented. It repeats a different ideology entirely, and our failure to identify it prevents us from opposing it.

Puritanism, in the popular imagination, is a simple affair: a joyless cult of self-denial, of stripped churches and banned entertainments, of men in tall hats glowering at the slightest expression of pleasure. This caricature seems to place it at an immense distance from the Republican America of conspicuous consumption, of SUVs and McMansions and tax cuts for the rich. What could the Founding Fathers' persecutors have to do with Dick Cheney? But Puritanism was never merely a matter of sobriety. It was the product of an economic transformation, and it arose to serve the interests of the class that transformation empowered.

In early 17th-century England, the remnants of the feudal state still functioned in a manner that bears an uncanny resemblance to 20th-century social democracy. The state served the monarchy and the clergy, certainly, but it also regulated economic exploitation. As R.H. Tawney documented in Religion and the Rise of Capitalism, published in 1926, Charles I attempted to nationalise key industries, control foreign exchange, and prosecute lords who evicted peasants from their land, employers who underpaid their workers, and magistrates who denied poor relief. The state, in other words, saw itself as the guarantor of a social order in which the powerful were constrained by obligation and the weak were protected by law.

This model collapsed because it could not withstand the economic revolution that swept over it. Over the course of 150 years, international commercial companies concentrated financial power on a scale the medieval world had never witnessed, subjecting the old industrial organisation to a new money-power. Commerce and finance, not industry, drove the transformation. Monarchs became the puppets of financiers. In England, the dissolution of the monasteries catalysed a massive seizure of wealth by a new commercial class: land enclosure, mass eviction, land speculation, and the development of sophisticated financial markets experimenting in futures and arbitrage — all the vices we now associate with the Age of Enron.

The early Reformers had furiously denounced this exploitation. The first Protestant theologians preached charity, justice, and the punishment of those who enriched themselves at the expense of the poor. This spirit persisted among the settlers of New England, but in England it was crushed. Puritanism became the religion of the new commercial classes: traders, money lenders, bankers, industrialists. Calvin gave them what the old order could not: a theological justification of commerce. Capitalism, for the first time, could glorify God.

The logical steps are not hard to trace. From Calvin's doctrine of individual purification came an idealisation of personal responsibility before God, which became a theory of individual rights, reversing the traditional scheme of Christian virtues. By the mid-17th century, most English Puritans saw poverty as moral failure, not misfortune, and wealth as a blessing that rewarded energy and will. Individualism in religion led to individualist morality, which led to the disparagement of the social fabric. Then the Puritans conflated religious and commercial callings: the preacher Richard Steele observed in 1684 that the tradesman serves God in his calling. Business success became proof of spiritual grace. Joseph Lee, writing over a century before Adam Smith, anticipated the invisible hand, claiming that the advancement of private persons serves the public — by which he meant that men of property should be free to destroy everyone else's advancement.

Tawney characterised the Puritans as early converts to what he called "administrative nihilism": the minimal state. Business affairs should be settled by businessmen, unhampered by antiquated morality. They formulated a new theory of social obligation: helping the poor created idleness and spiritual dissolution. There is, it must be said, one difference between the Puritans and Bush's people. The Puritans worshipped production; Bush's party worships consumption. But this is the same disease with a different symptom. Tawney's words capture them both: the world exists not to be enjoyed, but to be conquered; only its conqueror deserves the name of Christian.

The Levellers and the Diggers, who remained true to the original Reformation spirit of justice and communal obligation, were violently suppressed. The pursuit of adulterers and sodomites served as a distraction for the impoverished lower classes, directing their rage away from those who had dispossessed them and towards those who had transgressed against God. Ronan Bennett's novel Havoc in its Third Year, set in the 1630s, functions as a parable. Its characters are obsessed with terrorists — Irish and Jesuit — with homosexuality, with sexual license. They demand vicious punishment of moral deviance. They disparage public support for the poor. Swap black suits for grey, and these characters walk straight out of Bush's America.

Why has this ideology resurfaced in 2004? Because it must. The enrichment of the elite and the impoverishment of the lower classes require a justifying ideology if they are to be sustained. In the United States, that ideology must be religious. Bush's government is driven back to Puritanism by historical necessity. To understand what we are confronting, we must look not to the 1930s but to the 1630s.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_105
body:
A man called Mohamed, if you please,
must hand out the keys with ease,
while taking them back
from nations that lack
the sense to say "thank you" and "no, please."

There is something faintly absurd about the position in which the director-general of the International Atomic Energy Agency now finds himself. With one hand he is charged with stripping the world of nuclear weapons capability. With the other he is required to distribute the materials and expertise necessary to build it. Mohamed El Baradei, like his predecessor Hans Blix, is expected to serve two masters whose instructions flatly contradict each other. The agency he runs is supposed to prevent proliferation while simultaneously promoting the spread of the very technology that makes proliferation possible. This is not a paradox that can be managed. It is a contradiction that can only be resolved in one direction, and the direction, as we have seen in Iran, is towards the bomb.

The roots of this absurdity lie in a speech delivered to the United Nations in December 1953 by President Dwight D Eisenhower. "Atoms for Peace" was, by all accounts, a genuine expression of postwar optimism. The atomic genie was out of the bottle, Eisenhower reasoned, so the best that could be done was to channel its energies towards human betterment. He proposed to beat the nuclear sword into a ploughshare. The United States and the other nuclear powers would contribute fissionable materials to a new international agency, which would distribute them to "power-starved" regions of the world. The gift would serve as an incentive: nations receiving it would forswear the development of weapons.

The dream was magnificent, and it was taken seriously. A contemporaneous newspaper article, typical of dozens published at the time, promised that atomic cars would soon replace the internal combustion engine, that coal mining would become a relic of a primitive past, and that humanity stood on the threshold of an "earthly paradise" powered by clean and inexhaustible energy. The IAEA was founded in 1957 to realise this vision. Its statute, and later the Non-Proliferation Treaty of 1968, encoded a dual purpose: the agency would both prevent the spread of nuclear weapons and accelerate the spread of civilian nuclear technology. The two goals were assumed to be complementary. They were, in fact, mutually exclusive.

This program made sense only while the dream was still dreamable. The moment states began to translate civilian capacity into military capability, the contradiction became lethal. And they did so almost immediately. Every state that has developed nuclear weapons in the past 30 years—Israel, South Africa, India, Pakistan, North Korea, Iraq—did so by diverting resources from civilian nuclear programs, sometimes with direct assistance from the Atoms for Peace initiative. India's bomb was assembled with help from Canada, which supplied a research reactor; the United States, which provided heavy water; Germany, France, Norway and the United Kingdom, all of which contributed materials, training or technical expertise. Pakistan's nuclear program received parallel assistance, much of it channelled through civilian cooperation agreements. The great powers, in other words, were equipping nations for total war in the name of peace.

The result is that roughly 20 countries now possess the technical capacity to become weapons states within months, thanks to foreign civilian nuclear assistance. Russia was not merely permitted but obligated, under the provisions of the Non-Proliferation Treaty, to ship uranium and bomb-making technology to Iran. The treaty that is supposed to prevent proliferation is the same treaty that makes it legally unavoidable.

Iran is enriching uranium and producing heavy water. Both processes are legitimate for power generation. Both can be repurposed for weapons with minimal effort. Enriching uranium to reactor grade requires thousands of centrifuge passes; enriching it to bomb grade requires only additional passes through the same cascades. The infrastructure is identical. The difference is a matter of time and political will. The NPT gives Iran both the materials and the legal cover for a weapons program. If you want a bomb, the obvious strategy is to sign the treaties, join the IAEA, and use your entitlements. This is not a loophole. It is the treaty functioning as designed.

Iran's motives are not mysterious. Israel possesses a nuclear arsenal of some 200 warheads, and is protected by the United States in its refusal to sign the NPT or submit to inspections. The US and the UK have abandoned their own disarmament obligations under the treaty and have openly discussed the pre-emptive use of nuclear weapons against non-nuclear states. American military bases surround Iran on every side. And then there is the lesson of North Korea: that possessing the bomb grants impunity. Saddam Hussein did not have one, and he was hanged. Kim Jong-il did, and he was offered bilateral talks. If Iran is not seeking nuclear weapons, it has profoundly misunderstood the way the world works.

El Baradei can only beg. The treaty he is charged with enforcing gives him no authority to stop legally permitted enrichment. He can ask Iran to accept "voluntary agreements" and "confidence-building measures." He can express concern. He can issue reports. But he cannot compel, because the legal framework under which he operates simultaneously guarantees Iran's right to enrich and prohibits him from interfering with that right. The man charged with preventing proliferation is the same man charged with facilitating it. He is, in effect, asked to destroy the world in order to save it.

The danger is not abstract. The official nuclear powers have junked their obligations under the NPT while the non-nuclear powers exploit its provisions. The result is the spectre of a Middle East nuclear cascade. If Iran acquires the bomb, Saudi Arabia, Egypt and Turkey will follow. The cascading effects are not hard to imagine: Hezbollah, emboldened by an Iranian umbrella, strikes Israel; Israel, fearing annihilation, turns on Iran; a second nuclear arms race pulls in everyone else. The architecture of deterrence that prevented catastrophe during the cold war depended on two rational adversaries. It cannot survive a world of a dozen nuclear states, some of them governed by factions that regard martyrdom as a strategic asset.

The conclusion is obvious but unspoken. You cannot phase out nuclear weapons without phasing out nuclear power. The two are conjoined twins. Every reactor is a potential bomb factory. Every enrichment facility is a weapons laboratory waiting for a change of political direction. Every shipment of nuclear fuel is a contribution to a future arsenal. The IAEA's dual mission is not a creative tension between competing goods. It is an engine of proliferation, dressed in the language of restraint.

We need a new nuclear treaty, based on grim realities rather than Eisenhower's utopian fantasy. It must prohibit the production and trade of fissile materials for any purpose, military or civilian. It must mandate the dismantling of existing enrichment and reprocessing facilities. It must replace the impossible dream of atoms for peace with the necessary reality of atoms under lock and key. The alternative is to continue with the present arrangement, in which the head of the IAEA destroys the world in order to save it, and calls it balance.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_106
body:
Why does a country full of brilliant people so often end up choosing between two unimpressive men? The answer is simple: because unimpressive men keep getting elected. This year, as four years ago, the progressive establishment in the United States is instructing voters to choose the safer, duller option. John Kerry may be about as inspiring as a parking lot on a wet Sunday in Detroit, but he is, we are told, the only man who can defeat George Bush. And so, once again, the left is being asked to hold its nose and vote for the man who makes its heart sink.

I should lay out the case honestly, because it has real merit. Nearly every major progressive voice — the Guardian, the Nation magazine, even Noam Chomsky — has urged swing-state voters to back Kerry. Their logic is straightforward. The race is so tight that even a few thousand Nader votes in states like Ohio or Florida could hand Bush the election, just as they are widely believed to have done in 2000. Republicans, who understand this arithmetic perfectly, have even funnelled money to Nader's campaign in the hope of drawing votes from Kerry. The world, they say, cannot afford another four years of Bush.

They are right to be frightened. Bush has launched two unnecessary wars, threatened dozens of nations, shredded international treaties and domestic regulations, granted corporations a licence to accelerate climate change, assaulted civil liberties across the globe, and dismantled the idea that the state should tax the rich and help the poor. The world would be safer without him. This is not a trivial matter. It is not a game. The people who are urging us to vote for Kerry are doing so because they can see what Bush is doing to the world, and they are desperate to stop him.

The Guardian argued in a leader that Kerry's flaws are overshadowed by Bush's neoconservative agenda and catastrophic war-making. The Nation published an open letter to Nader asserting that 2004 is not 2000, that Bush's defeat is critical, that the stakes are too high for vanity campaigns. These are serious people making serious arguments, and they deserve to be taken seriously.

But here is the counter-punch. Both publications said the same thing in 2000. The Nation's Eric Alterman blamed Gore's defeat on Nader's "megalomania." The Guardian, before the 2000 election, called Gore the "safe, wise choice," despite conceding that he was neither exciting nor visionary. Similar warnings have appeared in nearly every modern presidential election. The pattern is unmistakable: every four years, the same voices tell us that this election is different, that the circumstances are exceptional, that the Republican candidate is uniquely dangerous, and that we must therefore abandon our principles and vote for the Democrat. The result is that the US electoral system — built on patronage, corruption, and media fear — continues to produce dreadful candidates, and therefore continues to generate "exceptional circumstances." There will always be a reason to vote for the bad to stop the terrible. Only a leader willing to overturn the system and reintroduce democracy will end the cycle. Voting for Kerry to stop Bush in 2004 guarantees the same dilemma in 2008, and onward indefinitely, until the system that produces these choices is itself confronted.

Such systemic change requires political courage: the courage to take on the corporations that buy elections and the media that sets the boundaries of debate. Kerry displayed genuine courage in Vietnam, but he has shown a total absence of it on the campaign trail. He has said that "even knowing what we now know," he would still have voted to authorise the Iraq war. His national security adviser, James Rubin, told the Washington Post that Kerry would "in all probability" have attacked Iraq by now if he were president. His fundraising prowess is not a democratic triumph but a corporate investment: his backers fund him because they expect compliance, and they are unlikely to be wrong. When Kerry gets his orders, he reports for duty.

It is fatuous to suppose that Kerry could oversee democratic reform. He is a frightened, flinching man, who has made the system work for him by working for the system. He knows that it would destroy him if he turned against it. He lacks the charisma or popular enthusiasm of a Hugo Chávez, who at least had the courage to challenge the structures that impoverished his people. A vote for Kerry is not merely a vote against Bush. It is a vote for the survival of the system that made Bush possible.

Now consider Ralph Nader. I disagree with him on plenty of things — on trade, for instance, where his protectionism sometimes slides into nativism. But no one can deny his courage. His decision to run despite near-universal progressive opposition is both brave and foolhardy. He has spent his career fighting corporations and enduring media attacks, and he has been willing to take on the Anti-Defamation League, which he accuses of smearing critics of Israeli policy as antisemites — something no other US politician has dared to do. He will not win. But the point is that systemic change must begin now, not in an endlessly postponed future, and the better Nader performs, the faster the movement for change will grow.

The Nation has suggested that Nader would have more impact as a "public citizen," advocating for open debates and rallying voters behind progressive Democrats. But what incentive would Democrats have to listen to him then? He holds leverage over these cowering creatures only while they fear he will take their votes. Remove that fear, and they will ignore him as comprehensively as they ignored him before.

I acknowledge the differences between Kerry and Bush. Bush is active, Kerry passive. Kerry wants high defence spending but would be more reluctant to attack other nations. He wants tax cuts but is less willing to gut health and education. He wants lower oil prices but won't help corporations open the Arctic Wildlife Refuge. These differences matter. They are not nothing.

But is this difference enough to justify abandoning the only current electoral effort to democratise US politics? No. The progressive establishment's plea amounts to asking voters to trade liberty for security, democracy for expediency. The voters should ignore them.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/validation/1/validation_5
body:
Nick Raynsford, the planning minister, is alleged to have helped cover up a decision to allow a listed building in Oxford to be moved to make way for a business school funded by the arms broker Wafic Said. He has an unchallengeable alibi. At the time the decision was taken, he was in Egypt, leading a trade mission. He was nowhere near the planning office, nowhere near Oxford, nowhere near the building in question. His defenders have been able to bat away every accusation with a single, devastating fact: he was out of the country. But the real question is not whether Nick Raynsford was involved in the Oxford decision. The real question is what a planning minister was doing leading a trade delegation to Egypt in the first place. His alibi, in other words, is more incriminating than the alleged crime.

The trade mission he led to Egypt in 1998 consisted of representatives from 27 corporations, including Balfour Beatty, Enron, Laing, Mott MacDonald, Thames Water and WS Atkins. They were taken to see the Toshka project, a $90bn scheme to divert water from the Nile into the Western Desert, condemned both by environmentalists, who point to the devastation it will cause to fragile desert ecosystems and to the Nile delta, and by financial consultants, who observe that it makes no economic sense whatsoever. It is the kind of project which, if it were proposed in Britain, would fall squarely within the regulatory remit of the planning minister. The man who is supposed to police such developments was instead touring the site as a salesman.

The major contract to emerge from the Toshka project was awarded not to a British company but to Kvaerner, the Norwegian engineering firm. This might seem to make the trade mission a failure, at least from the perspective of the British taxpayer who funded it. But it begins to make sense when you discover that Kvaerner had seconded more employees into the Department of the Environment, Transport and the Regions than any other company. The regulator, in other words, had been captured by the regulated. The department which was supposed to oversee the construction industry had been so thoroughly infiltrated by one of its members that it was effectively running the show.

This helps to explain the extraordinary conflict of interest at the heart of Raynsford's brief. He is both the minister for planning, which means he is the regulator of development, and the minister for construction, which means he is the promoter of development. These two roles are irreconcilable. You cannot simultaneously police an industry and champion it. And when the conflict arises, Raynsford resolves it, invariably, in industry's favour. He told Building magazine that his job was "creating a climate in which the industry can do well." This is not the language of a regulator. It is the language of a lobbyist.

The evidence is not hard to find. Raynsford sponsored a PR campaign called "Minerals 98," funded by quarrying firms, which lobbied against the proposed roadstone tax. He argued against out-of-town parking taxes at Labour's 1998 local government conference. He promised the British Council of Shopping Centres that he would speed up the planning process for retail schemes. He watered down the energy efficiency rules for new homes, dismissing them as "an undue burden on builders." In every case, he used his regulatory powers to advance the interests of the industry he was supposed to be controlling.

But the gravest allegation is this. Raynsford, it is claimed, persuaded Tony Blair to back the Ilisu dam in Turkey. The dam will drown the ancient city of Hasankeyf and force some 78,000 Kurds from their homes, in what appears to be a continuation of the Turkish government's ethnic cleansing programme. In September 1999, Raynsford led a trade mission to Turkey, accompanying a consortium fronted by Balfour Beatty, which was seeking £4bn in earthquake-rebuilding contracts. Balfour Beatty also hopes to build the Ilisu dam. Three months after the trade mission, the government announced that it was "minded to grant" export credit guarantees for the project. We are entitled to ask whether British contracts were made conditional on support for the dam.

The Pergau dam scandal, you may recall, involved the Conservative government misdirecting £200m to secure contracts for Balfour Beatty. The Ilisu dam involves the Labour government misdirecting £200m to secure contracts for Balfour Beatty. The only difference is that this time the project will assist the ethnic cleansing of the Kurds rather than merely lining the pockets of a corrupt foreign regime. But then, as we have seen, the construction industry has few more zealous friends in government than the man who is supposed to regulate it.

And so we return to the Oxford business school. Everything Nick Raynsford says about it is probably true. There was nothing unusual about the decision. It is the kind of decision his department makes every week. The outrage is not that he covered it up, but that he didn't need to. Turning a blind eye to environmental and heritage destruction on behalf of the construction industry is not an aberration. It is the daily work of the very man appointed to prevent it.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_119
body:
I have been coming to America for 40 years. I studied there, reported from there, made friends and connections there, married an American. By every reasonable measure, I knew the country. I did not know it. Perhaps America did not fully know itself, until September 11 and the Afghan war forced it into an act of self-examination such as no external event had compelled since Pearl Harbor. What I discovered, spending Christmas in a remote corner of Vermont, is that nothing has changed. Everything is more so.

This is the thesis, and it is perhaps an audacious one to advance from so small a vantage point. Vermont is not America, though Vermonters like to think it contains the best of what America might be. It is a liberal state, sparsely populated, sending a self-described socialist to Congress, with little affection for George W. Bush. But from this vantage point, three months after the attack, one can see certain national traits not merely intact but intensified, clarified, stripped of the ambiguity that used to surround them.

Consider, first, consideration itself. Americans were always considerate. Now they are super-caring. The attack was collectively shared in a way that no British event I can recall has been shared in my lifetime. Three months on, the shock still reverberates. There has been no stoical sloughing-off, no brisk return to business as usual. People still blunder about searching for meaning, and their response is to care more tenderly — for the bereaved, for firemen, for strangers, for each other. The kindness that was always there has acquired a desperate quality, as though tenderness were the only adequate response to an event that remains, in some fundamental sense, inexplicable.

Second, self-confidence. There is no whining, no self-pity. Self-sufficiency is second nature, as it always was, but now it has a harder edge. The American certainties — that this is the greatest country on earth, that its values are self-evidently right, that its system of government is the last best hope of mankind — these certainties, which were always maddening to Osama bin Laden and his kind, have redoubled. What is striking is the return of humor. For a period of solemnity after the attacks, humor seemed to have been suspended. But it came back. I heard Garrison Keillor telling jihad jokes on the radio, and it struck me that Islam has been quietly dropped from the protective canopy of political correctness. You can joke about the mullahs now, in a way you could not have joked about any other religion's extremists a year ago. This is not intolerance. It is the resumption of normal American irreverence, directed at a target that has earned it.

Third, patriotism. Flags always hung in Vermont yards. But now patriotism is far more intense. The irony is worth noting: Vermont sends a socialist to Congress and has little time for Bush, yet the patriotic response to September 11 is unquestioning.
