This is a tourist-free area in winter, with very local papers. The Valley News of White River Junction, not normally a paper that leads with foreign affairs, now carries Hamid Karzai's swearing-in as its front-page story, and inside runs news of Yasser Arafat's visit to Bethlehem. This is not new internationalism. It is a shedding of indifference toward the world the threat comes from. America's role, as most Americans see it, is simply to protect America. They do not see why this should be controversial, and they are not ashamed of it.

Fourth, constitutionalism. Here the change is more marked and more troubling. Even liberal Vermont acquaintances show only modest objection to Bush's military tribunals and the curtailments of due process that have followed the attacks. The government cites Lincoln's suspension of habeas corpus and FDR's internment of Japanese-Americans as precedent. This seems to satisfy people. The constitutional scruples that one might have expected from a state that prides itself on its civil libertarian traditions are largely absent. There is a mild unease, but it does not run deep. The threat is felt to be real, and the measures taken are felt to be proportionate. This is the area where America has become most "more so" — more willing to trade liberty for security, more trusting of executive power, more ready to accept that the Constitution is not a suicide pact.

Fifth, there is the divergence between domestic politics and foreign policy. Domestic politics continues normally. Budget arguments proceed, left and right contend, taxation debates rage, positioning for the 2002 elections is well under way. But in foreign affairs, politics has virtually ceased. America has become easy to lead. Alarmingly so. Even those who detested Bush, who doubted his legitimacy, who scoffed at his intellect, now trust him because there seems no alternative. He vows to "get the job done," that archetypal American phrase, and the country believes him. The congressional dissent that might have been expected has melted away. The press, which might have been more sceptical, has fallen into line. This is not the suspension of politics that follows a declaration of war, because there has been no declaration of war. It is something more insidious: a voluntary abdication of the critical faculty, in the face of a threat that is genuinely felt but imperfectly understood.

What one is left with, at Christmas in Vermont, is a sense of casual fatalism. Friends joke about being "blown to pieces." There is a sense that fate has taken over, and that America must do what it must do: extinguish al-Qaida. American idealism, historically fickle in post-war diplomacy — quick to lose interest, quick to declare victory and go home — now has a purpose everyone agrees on: saving America itself. What this means for the world is unclear. But clearer than it has been since Jefferson what the world means for America: safety from sea to shining sea, and maybe not beyond.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_13
body:
The politics of law and order is entirely about spin. Not partly, not substantially, but entirely. This is not to say that every impression conveyed happens to be false. Some of the raw material happens to be correct. Crime has roughly doubled over 20 years. The public does genuinely want heavier sentencing. But the material politicians handle, the stuff they shape and project and argue over, is propaganda, not fact. It is the manufacture of perception, the curation of fear, the deployment of numbers chosen not for what they reveal but for what they can be made to suggest. In no other area of political conflict does impression so completely eclipse reality.

Consider the statistical warfare that passes for debate. The home secretary announces that crime has fallen by the largest amount in a century. The shadow home secretary retorts that violent crime has risen to record levels under this government. Both claims are relatively defensible. It depends what years you measure, what categories you include, and how you shade the figures. A government that wants to show success will point to burglary and car crime, which have fallen. An opposition that wants to show failure will point to street robbery and assault, which have risen. The voter cannot possibly parse this. An audit of the government's crime figures by Nick Davies and Felicity Lawrence, published in the Guardian, illustrated the mixed picture with characteristic precision: burglary down significantly, car theft down, violent crime down modestly, but street robbery up, clear-up rates stagnant, and convictions up. Here was a portrait of a society in which some things were getting better and some worse, and the aggregate told you almost nothing useful. But sophistication, however true, will always be outgunned by simplicity, however false. A headline that says "crime soars" will always defeat an analysis that says "crime is a complex phenomenon with divergent trends." That is the nature of the medium, and the politicians know it.

The public, meanwhile, is startlingly uninformed. A national crime survey shows that the public believes judges are too lenient and out of touch. This is an article of faith so widely held as to be unchallengeable. But the same survey reveals that over half of respondents underestimate actual sentences by at least 30%. They believe that half of convicted rapists go to prison, when the real figure is 97%. Similar misconceptions surround burglary and mugging sentences, where the public imagines suspended sentences or community service where years of custody are routinely imposed. These grotesque misperceptions are the foundation of the judiciary's "soft touch" reputation. It is an image brainlessly cultivated by the tabloid press, which distorts individual cases into archetypes, which then seeps into ministerial critique and policy. Ministers do not correct the misperception. They exploit it. They position themselves as the champions of a public whose ignorance they have every reason to nurture.

The legislative record tells its own story. The previous Conservative government passed a criminal justice act every year, until later statutes began undoing earlier ones. Each act was a response to a perceived crisis, each crisis was amplified by media, and each response was designed to produce a headline. The current government's record is similarly mixed. Curfew orders and anti-social behaviour orders, though reasonable in design, have failed in practice. They are widely breached, inconsistently applied, and have become symbols of a system that announces resolve and delivers confusion. The latest reform package, allowing judges to take account of previous convictions in sentencing, is a rational idea. It addresses the analysis, well supported by evidence, that a small population of roughly 100,000 repeat offenders commits most crime. If you can identify and punish this group more effectively, you might make a genuine difference. The idea will spin well, of course. The opposition will spin against it just as easily, denouncing it as too little too late, or as an infringement of judicial independence, or as a recycled Tory policy, depending on which line plays best on the evening news.

Three conclusions follow from this, none of which the spinning addresses.

First, the best policies cost money. The current reform package is priced at around £600m. Is this sufficient to fund new custodial regimes, accelerated court hearings, and increased police manpower? Plainly it is not. A larger police force is the single most persuasive remedy across all the confusing statistics. More officers on the street, more detection, more consistent community policing — these are the measures that command the widest expert support and the most credible evidence base. Yet the home secretary has conspicuously failed to meet recruitment promises. The question is whether the Treasury is genuinely committed to the investment this would require, or whether the announcements are mere propaganda timed to an upcoming election, pencilled in, let us say, for May 2005. The pattern is familiar: a dramatic initiative, a press conference, a headline, and then the money quietly fails to materialise or is diverted to other priorities.

Second, there is a hole at the heart of the crime agenda. While politicians offer drugs as an explanation for crime — they never tire of pointing out that a high proportion of acquisitive crime is drug-related — they refuse to address drug laws as a focus for reform despite considerable expert pressure. Decriminalising cannabis would eliminate a category of crime at a stroke, free up police time, unclog the courts, and redirect resources towards the hard drugs that genuinely drive criminal behaviour. The spinners dare not look at it. They calculate that any mention of decriminalisation will be twisted by opponents into a soft-on-drugs headline, and so the entire subject is quarantined from serious political discussion, however much the evidence accumulates, however many police chiefs and senior judges privately concede the point.

Third, prisons keep multiplying. Britain has the highest imprisonment rate in Europe. England and Wales rank joint first among industrialised nations in crime as experienced by victims. No accountable politician will connect these two trends. The juxtaposition is devastating: we lock up more people than any comparable society, and we suffer more crime than almost any comparable society. The implication — that imprisonment is not the answer to the crime problem in the way the public has been sold — is one no minister or shadow minister can afford to voice. Recent critiques of the prison system from the lord chief justice, the inspector of prisons, and the director of the prison service have been clinical and passionate, documenting the failure of prisons to rehabilitate, the scandal of overcrowding, the waste of human potential. But the electoral auctioneers dare not change the terms of debate. They compete only to sound tougher, never to sound wiser.

The consequence of all this is a politics in which the spinners can only appeal to the lowest denominator of opinion, however ignorant. They must play to the misperception, not the reality. They must compete on the territory the tabloids have defined, not the territory the evidence suggests. And the spinner, in this as in every contest, always, more or less, lies. He may lie with statistics, he may lie with implication, he may lie by the careful selection of what he chooses to mention and what he chooses to omit. But the lie is structural, not accidental. It is built into the system by the refusal to confront the public with the truth.

The beginning of wisdom in the politics of law and order would be for someone to start the arduous business of addressing the truth. This would require courage of a kind that is presently invisible in either front bench. It would mean telling the public that their perceptions of sentencing are wrong, that prisons are not working, that drug laws need fundamental reconsideration, that more police cost more money, and that the simplistic auction in toughness has produced nothing but a legislative merry-go-round. Nobody can any longer sort one spinner from another. The claims and counter-claims cancel each other out, and what remains is a residue of public cynicism that is, on this subject at least, entirely justified. The only way through is for someone to break the cycle, risk the headlines, and say what is actually the case. Whether any politician will judge this worth the price is a question that, on present evidence, answers itself.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_16
body:
It is hard to believe, looking at the distance travelled, that the Northern Ireland peace process might yet fail. Hard, but not impossible. The end game, as everyone now discovers, is the hardest part. It was always going to be. The conflict is no longer about power or religion, or even about the constitutional status of the province in any form that would be recognised by the people who began killing each other 30 years ago. It is about attitudes toward mutual surrender. It is about whether two traditions, each steeped in the conviction that surrender is the prerogative of the other side, can bring themselves to perform it simultaneously. What makes the present crisis so dangerous is that both sides have made sincere and substantial concessions. Two forms of real sincerity are now ranged against each other. This is why the challenge may defeat them. For when sincerity meets sincerity across an unbridgeable gap of mistrust, there is no obvious formula by which compromise can be extracted from conviction.

On the republican side, there is a sincere belief that the war is over. This needs to be stated plainly, because it is often denied or doubted by people who have every reason to doubt it but who are, in this case, wrong. Sinn Fein-IRA, led by Gerry Adams and Martin McGuinness, has shifted onto a political track that would have been unimaginable to the men who planted bombs in Birmingham or Enniskillen. The IRA has asserted its "total" commitment to a "permanent peace." These are not words that come easily to an organisation whose entire historical identity has been built on the proposition that armed struggle is the only authentic expression of Irish nationhood. The Adams argument runs as follows: republicanism has given up its constitutional claim to a united Ireland by accepting the principle of consent, is helping to run the country through the institutions established under the Good Friday agreement, and talks to the British government on terms of something approaching equality. Their U-turn is credible precisely because of how long and how bloodily they resisted it. An organisation that fought for 30 years does not reverse course for tactical convenience. It does so because the leadership has concluded, after everything, that the military option is exhausted.

From this perspective, decommissioning is achievable but beside the point. The de facto end of violence is what matters. The IRA has called a ceasefire. It has observed it, by and large, for several years. It has permitted its political wing to enter government alongside unionists. It has engaged, however glancingly, with General de Chastelaine's decommissioning body. To the republican mind, the demand that weapons be physically handed over before the political institutions can function is a unionist trap dressed up as a democratic principle. The guns are silent. What more does anyone need?

The unionist position deserves equal recognition, and has not always received it from commentators who view the process from a comfortable distance. Unionists have shown sincerity of a depth that their detractors rarely acknowledge. They have accepted republicans into power. They have started running the government of Northern Ireland alongside men who, within living memory, were directing a campaign of murder against their community. They have overcome vast obstacles in the unionist psyche — the deep, almost constitutional suspicion of any arrangement that appears to legitimise the representatives of violent Irish nationalism. They have done so under pressure from hate-filled supporters who regard the Good Friday agreement as betrayal, and from parts of the London press that have been hoping for failure with an intensity that sometimes borders on the pathological. David Trimble has shown statesmanship of a high order in manoeuvring past these historical negatives. He has taken his people further than most of them wanted to go, and further than any of his predecessors would have dared. Unionists have already lived with procrastination over decommissioning that would have been unthinkable as a concession even five years ago. They will swallow most of the Patten report on the RUC, a reform that goes to the heart of their sense of identity and security, because they understand that a police force cannot remain the instrument of one community in a society that aspires to govern itself jointly.

The outsider's temptation, not steeped in the bitter particularities of Ulster politics, is to say that unionists should take one more stride. Let the institutions function. Let Trimble stay in government with Sinn Fein even if decommissioning is not delivered by the May 22 deadline. The argument has a certain logic. Having swallowed the camel, are unionists really straining at the gnat? They have accepted the principle of power-sharing with unreconstructed republicans. They have accepted that the IRA retains its arsenal. They have accepted that the war, for the moment, is over only on the IRA's own say-so. Is the physical handover of a few semtex blocks and rifles really the point on which the entire settlement should turn?

There is a further argument, often deployed with moral fervour, that to insist on Trimble's resignation if decommissioning is not delivered is to put blood on unionist hands. If the institutions collapse, the reasoning runs, the ceasefire may not hold. If the ceasefire does not hold, people will die. Therefore the unionists, by their intransigence over a symbolic issue, will have created the conditions for violence. This is a serious charge, and it deserves an answer.

It is not insensitive to talk about blame. On the contrary, it is irresponsible not to. In the hour of crisis, it is the republicans who are on trial. This is not because unionists are blameless or because their historical record is beyond reproach. It is because the specific question that now threatens the process is a question that only republicans can answer. Realpolitik has reached its limit. The British and Irish governments have done everything that diplomacy can do. They have cajoled, threatened, constructed elaborate formulae of words, set deadlines and extended them, brought in international figures of unimpeachable authority. General de Chastelaine has provided every possible facility for decommissioning to take place with dignity and without humiliation. What remains is an act of political will that only the IRA can perform.

Sinn Fein-IRA must take a decisive — though entirely incomplete — surrender. A token handover of weapons is substantively meaningless. Everyone knows this. The IRA will retain the vast bulk of its arsenal. The capacity to resume violence will remain. General de Chastelaine will certify what he has received, and the world will note that it is a fraction of what exists. But the gesture is politically crucial. It is the one thing that distinguishes a commitment to peace from a mere pause in war. It is the visible, tangible sign that the IRA has accepted the logic of the process into which its political leadership has entered. Without it, everything rests on promise. And in the real world, a promise is not enough.

This is the point that the romantic defenders of republican sensibility fail to grasp. They say that the IRA cannot be expected to surrender. They are right. Total surrender is not being asked for. They say that the IRA cannot be expected to humiliate itself. A token handover, conducted through the agreed mechanisms, would involve no humiliation. They say that the end of violence is what matters, and that decommissioning is a distraction. But the end of violence, to be believed, must be demonstrated. Words are cheap. They are cheaper still when they come from an organisation that has broken ceasefire before. The unbearable discomfort of a necessary gesture is required — not because it changes anything material, but because it proves that republicanism has entered the world it chose to belong to. The world of democratic politics, of compromise, of the ballot box, of the grubby and imperfect business of governing alongside people you do not trust and may never love.

Everything else is bunker piety. The insistence that the war is over but the weapons must stay is bunker piety. The claim that decommissioning is a unionist obsession unworthy of republican engagement is bunker piety. The posture that says, in effect, we have given enough and will give no more, while the institutions of government hang by a thread and the deadline approaches — that too is bunker piety. It is the language of the bunker, not the assembly. It belongs to the past that the Good Friday agreement was supposed to bury. If that agreement is to survive, the gesture must come. Not because it solves everything, but because without it, nothing else can begin to be solved.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_11
body:
All parties now agree that ideology is dead. The word itself has become a term of abuse, as dirty in the mouth of a Tory struggling through a leadership contest as it is in the rhetoric of a Labour prime minister delivering a major speech on the future of public services. Nobody wants to be caught wielding it. The very sound of it carries the stale odour of the 1980s, of clenched fists and clause four, of miners and mass privatisation, of a politics that most politicians and quite a lot of voters are heartily relieved to have left behind. And who would not be pleased to see the back of it? But there is a price, and it is not a small one. The death of ideology has made government both more necessary and harder to explain. Politics has become messier, more conditional, less susceptible to the kind of simple framing that once enabled ordinary voters to locate themselves on a map of political possibility. When the defining mantra of the left said public good and private bad, and the right said roughly the opposite, politics was easy to make sense of. You knew where you stood, because where you stood was what defined you. Now everything is more complicated. The silver thread that linked and determined everything has gone.

While the practice of the trade has had no alternative but to recognise this, public understanding has not caught up. It strives to cling to the old simplicities. The mindset of ideology survives the passing of the ideological age. It is not merely in newspaper headlines, groping for the familiar matrix, that a centre-left government's plans for the delivery of health and education are seen as a private-sector takeover. I suspect most people in the country see it that way too. The vocabulary of reform is read through a lens ground decades ago. Any mention of the private sector in the context of a public service triggers a reflex that bypasses thought entirely. Privatization, the word itself, is deployed as though it were a single self-evident accusation requiring no further elaboration.

For this the government is largely to blame. In its first term, feeling its way forward, it was not straight. It wanted to please all the people all the time, which is the classic recipe for pleasing nobody. It tended to reassure the business world with one spin about innovation and competition, while telling its own constituency, in gentler language, that nothing fundamental would change. Its attitude to the health service covered a gamut of promises from tough to tender. To business it spoke the language of markets and efficiency. To its base it spoke the language of the NHS safe in its hands. These were not necessarily incompatible messages, but they were never reconciled, and the result was a cacophony that left everyone uncertain about what the government actually believed. Its replacement weapon for ideology was "what works". But this became its philosophy well before it knew what worked in either health or education. It knew that what the Tories had done, especially in the NHS, did not work. It was far less sure how to replace it. The phrase was a placeholder dressed up as a principle, a confession of ignorance elevated to the status of a doctrine.

There remains a lot of confusion. Important differences are still not widely apprehended, partly because ministers' own schemes reflect the genuine complication of post-ideological government, and partly because the producer unions have an interest in making things seem more threatening than they are. Enlisting private-sector skills and money to develop surgical centres or turn round failing schools is not the same as privatisation. Very little of the public sector is capable of being run by the private sector in any case. Instituting regimes of competition or "contestability" is not an assault on public-service principles but a minimal necessity for raising them to the best-practice levels everyone is entitled to expect. This is standard practice across the continental European public sectors. In France, in Germany, in Holland, the involvement of non-state providers in the delivery of public services is unremarkable, uncontroversial, and utterly divorced from any suggestion that the principles of public provision are being abandoned. Only in Britain, where the ideological ghosts are louder than the living arguments, does the mere mention of it produce hysteria.

Yesterday, the prime minister got closer to clarifying some of this than he has done before. It was the most important speech he has made since re-election. Though the headline message will be his commitment not to "flinch" from union opposition, the real substance was more cogent than that. Some of the unions believe he is thirsting for a fight, even looking for a demonstration combat such as Mrs Thatcher had with the miners. This is a misreading. The speech was sober rather than militant. It reflected a late awakening to the danger that public services might be seen to fail, which is the one thing that could destroy everything this government exists to achieve. The contrast with Thatcher's combative style is instructive. She sought enemies because she believed in the virtue of vanquishing them. Blair speaks of not flinching not because he wants a war but because he has finally understood that the alternative to reform is catastrophe. This is not the language of the barricades. It comes from the last-chance saloon of British social democracy, which has rather suddenly woken up to the danger of its core proposition being seen to fail.

The speech sent three signals. First, there is no single grand blueprint. Different problems require different answers. Compared with some of the first-term rhetoric, the glorification of the private has actually receded. If more than half of local education authorities are described by Ofsted as either fairly or very successful, as they are, the sweeping abolition favoured by some reformers makes no sense. It makes as little sense to insist that only NHS units may tender to run the new surgical centres, just because they are being set up as a supplement to NHS hospitals rather than a replacement for them. The government has no single plan for public services. The temptation towards one, to which it was lured in the first term, has been resisted. There are different answers for different problems, and the attempt to impose a uniform solution would be a form of ideological thinking by another name.

Second, only a social-democratic government can be trusted to reform public services without simply enriching private business and ultimately harming the disadvantaged. This is a truth that needs stating, because it is the one thing that separates a serious reforming government from a Thatcherite one. The party with an incontestable belief in the primacy of public services is the only party that can reform their delivery in ways that do not merely profit private providers and deprive the less advantaged. As a matter of fact, this belief may be a little more widespread than it was. In the concurrent Conservative leadership contest, two of the candidates make almost everything of their commitment to health and education, and admit that failure in this field contributed most to the public's hatred of their party at the election. But the third candidate might yet win. Iain Duncan Smith is the voice of the hard Tory right, which flirts with the minimal state and much of which favours an American-style approach. With that line, he would be even less likely than his rivals to win the next election. But the state of Conservatism makes plain the danger that, without visible improvements brought about by progressive politicians, the public sector will become the source of yet deeper cynicism and despair.

Third, the challenge may be impossible to fully meet. The prime minister said yesterday that it was "as great as any that has faced a postwar government". We have to be aware that he has staked everything on outcomes that will, in many people's eyes, never be satisfactory. The famous glass, whether in school or hospital, is always in danger of being seen as half empty. Public expectations are high, and the very announcement of investment and reform raises them further. If the world economy, dragging Britain's with it, faces a prolonged downturn, not even the prudence of the chancellor will have been enough to preserve the budget surplus needed for the investment on which everything depends. The government has set itself a test it may not pass, however hard it works.

All one can say at the moment is that the prime minister has set out, without glamour or spin, on the defining stage of his engagement with the realities the people most value. That alone is worth recognising. It is worth supporting. The project has moved beyond the phase of presentation into something more difficult and more honest. Whether it succeeds or not, the attempt to govern without ideology while reforming the institutions ideology created is the central political challenge of our time. It has finally begun.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_12
body:
Between one term and the next, there comes a moment when memory ought to be audited. A government returning to office with a fresh mandate will soon enough begin erasing what it did in the first, not by denial but by the sheer forward momentum of new business. Before that happens, it is worth asking what power has done to the moral perception of ministers. I do not mean the familiar hardening of arteries that office brings, the drift from idealism to pragmatism that every government undergoes. I mean something more specific and more alarming: the closing of a gate inside the mind, whereby people who came in professing one set of beliefs find themselves enacting the precise opposite, and are genuinely unable to see that they have done so.

This government came to power with anti-racist credentials that were not merely rhetorical. They were substantive, and they were meant to be. Among the early actions was the abolition of a prior rule that had targeted migrant spouses, a regulation so manifestly discriminatory in its effect that its survival under the previous administration had been a standing disgrace. A landmark inquiry was convened into a racially motivated murder that had exposed the institutional rot inside the police service. Leading figures in the new administration treated racial justice as an article of faith. They spoke of it with the unqualified passion of people who could not imagine acting otherwise. It was, they said, part of what they were. To have suggested, in those early sunlit weeks, that within a few years they would be writing ethnic discrimination into statute, would have been dismissed as the ravings of someone who did not understand the essential decency of these particular ministers.

Yet this is what has happened. The first betrayal was quiet, almost technical, and that was its purpose. The government passed race relations legislation that, on its face, extended anti-discrimination protections across the public realm. But the immigration service was explicitly exempted. A ministerial order was issued authorising the differential treatment of unspecified nationalities. When this was pointed out, the responsible minister called it a "technicality." When the attack persisted, he labelled it "bizarre." The word is instructive. It reveals a man who has so thoroughly absorbed the righteousness of his own position that criticism of it registers not as disagreement but as a category error, something so outlandish as to be almost incomprehensible.

Then came the escalation. A second, more specific order was issued. This one did not deal in unspecified nationalities. It listed targeted ethnic groups by name: Kurd, Roma, Albanian, Tamil, Pontic Greek, Somali, Afghan. These groups, the order specified, were to face "more rigorous examination" as classes. They were to be presumed, by virtue of their ethnic identity, more likely to be illegal immigrants or bogus asylum-seekers. The presumption was not individual but collective. It attached not to conduct, not to evidence, not to any ascertainable fact about a particular person, but to the category itself.

Consider the vicious absurdity this enshrines. Several of the named groups have no corresponding passport or nation-state. Kurds do not carry a Kurdish passport. Roma do not arrive from a country called Romani. This means that identification of a person as belonging to such a group cannot rest on documentary evidence. It rests on appearance, on accent, on inference, on whatever the immigration officer thinks he sees when a person presents himself at the desk. What this amounts to, in any honest reading, is the enshrinement of racial-profiling logic into statute. The law now instructs officials to make judgments about people's ethnicity and to apply different standards of scrutiny accordingly. There is, moreover, a cruel sub-clause: if information needed for an application is not available in a language the applicant understands, officials need not provide it in one they do. The practical effect is that a person may be processed, scrutinised, and rejected in a language he cannot follow, and the law says this is perfectly acceptable.

The deception unfolded in two phases. During the bill's passage through parliament, ministers claimed that the power to discriminate between nationalities was intended only to operate in favour of vulnerable groups, Kosovan refugees being the example tendered. This was a lie. It was not a careless overstatement or a reasonable interpretation stretched too far. It was a lie, told to deflect criticism from a power that was always intended to be used in the opposite direction. After enactment, the responsible minister casually admitted that the order merely formalised long-standing practice. Immigration officers had been operating on these ethnic presumptions for years. The law was not introducing something new but ratifying something old. The minister expressed flippancy that anyone would object. The tone was not defensive but mildly puzzled, as though he could not quite fathom why anyone was making a fuss.

Three conclusions stare out from this history. First, ethnic selection is now systematized and official. It is not an informal practice that occasionally goes wrong. It is the law. A "race monitor" has been appointed, which sounds reassuring. But the role is retrospective. It examines aggregate data, not individual cases. It does not sit beside the immigration officer and ask why this particular Tamil was subjected to three hours of questioning while this particular Australian was waved through. It is a PR figurehead, designed to take the pressure off without changing the practice.

Second, this is political cowardice of a familiar but no less dismaying kind. The government is caving to the rhetoric of a hardline opposition figure who has made asylum the centrepiece of his appeal. Rather than contesting the discourse, ministers are collaborating in it. They are accepting the premise that asylum-seeking is presumptively fraudulent, that the system is being gamed, that the appropriate response is to make the process harsher and more discriminatory. In doing so, they validate the very discourse they once would have condemned. They do not fight the argument. They surrender to it and call it realism.

Third, something has rotted in the ruling party. The backbenches barely stirred. There was no revolt, no significant rebellion, no expression of dismay from the ranks of MPs who, in opposition, would have been apoplectic at the sight of a Tory government writing ethnic groups into a discrimination order. The party that once protested such things now acquiesces in them, and the acquiescence is made possible not by cynicism but by self-belief. The lead minister responsible is a self-styled anti-racist. He came into politics as a man of the left. He has spent his career believing that racial justice is part of his identity. And he genuinely cannot see the contradiction. This is the deeper corruption. It is not self-interested conspiracy. It is not cynical calculation. It is a wall of righteous certainty, the belief that because of who they are, because of what they stand for, because their intentions are good, they can do no wrong. A Tory government that introduced ethnic profiling would have been denounced by these people without mercy. They would have seen it clearly, because they would have seen it from the outside. From the inside, the same thing looks different. It looks like administration, like necessary regulation, like the unglamorous business of governing. The moral identity insulates them from the moral consequence of what they do.

This law is a disgrace. The bitter irony is that perhaps only a party convinced of its own anti-racism would have had the nerve to smuggle such ethnic punishment past the line separating decency from shame. A party with less certainty about its own virtue might have hesitated. It might have listened to the critics. It might have recognised, in the naming of Kurds and Roma and Tamils for special scrutiny, the unmistakable signature of the very thing it once existed to oppose. But certainty does not listen. It does not recognise. It does not see itself. That is what power has done, and the fresh mandate will soon enough bury it under new business, unless someone insists on remembering.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_127
body:
It is the kind of television that only insomniacs and the chronically nostalgic stumble across: late-night reruns of old Israeli television specials, preserved in grainy black and white, featuring kibbutz choirs in their Sunday best — men in open-necked work shirts, women in peasant skirts, all of them standing in a semicircle before a studio audience whose smiles look genuine rather than staged. They are singing the patriotic Hebrew folk melodies that once formed the soundtrack of a nation, those rousing, accordion-laced anthems of soil and sacrifice. It is easy to dismiss them now as cheesy, socialist-realist tableaus, relics of a vanished aesthetic that belongs more to the Soviet bloc than to the Middle East. But as Israel prepares to celebrate its 60th anniversary with modern street parties and beach barbecues, these old broadcasts offer something more than kitsch. They offer a window — and a profound one — into what Israel was, and what it has become. For the story they tell is of a country founded not as an imperialist project but as a desperate refuge for a stateless people, a place built on collectivist ideals that today's Israelis have quietly abandoned in favour of a privatized, self-enclosed "bubble" — and in that retreat, they have lost their urgency for peace.

Start with the critique that has become almost reflexive in certain quarters: that Israel was merely an act of western imperialism, a European enclave inserted into the Middle East, comparable to the white settlers of Australia or South Africa. It is a charge that carries a superficial plausibility, not least because the early Zionists were largely European and the project had the blessing, however conflicted, of the British empire. But it collides with a rather obvious fact: the ethnic composition of modern Israel. Walk down a street in Tel Aviv or Jerusalem and you will see faces that trace their origins to Morocco, Russia, Ethiopia, Yemen, Iraq, Kurdistan. Nearly half of Israel's Jews are not from Europe at all but from the Arab and Muslim world, or from Africa. This is not the demographic profile of a colonial settler movement. It is the profile of a refugee gathering.

The Zionist anthem "Ein Li Eretz Acheret" — "I Have No Other Land" — captures this with a starkness that is almost unbearable. The words speak not of conquest but of belonging, of a people for whom this sliver of earth is the only home they have. The tragic symmetry, of course, is that the Palestinians feel exactly the same way. They too have no other land. But the Jewish state was established not in a vacuum of European guilt but in the immediate aftermath of catastrophe: a mere three years separated the liberation of Auschwitz from the declaration of independence. The world had watched six million Jews murdered and finally, belatedly, recognized what should have been obvious all along — that a stateless people, denied the protection of any sovereign, had been left to the mercy of those who had none. To understand what drove the creation of Israel, consider the story of Yossi Harel, captain of the *Exodus*, the ship that carried 4,500 Holocaust survivors to Palestine in 1947. The British turned the vessel back and sent its passengers — emaciated, traumatized, stateless — to Germany, of all places. The image of Jewish refugees, survivors of the camps, being returned to the land of the Holocaust, was so potent it helped galvanize international support for a Jewish state. There is a moral distinction here that matters. The pioneers of America, Africa and Australasia were, by and large, landowners or fortune-seekers, people who already had a home and were looking for a second one, or for resources to exploit. The Jewish refugees of the 1940s were a homeless man desperate for a roof.

The old television specials capture something of that founding ethos. Early Israel was less like Rhodesia and more like East Germany — a small country where socialism was not merely a political preference but a state religion. The evidence of this collectivism was everywhere, and not just in the kibbutz choirs. The country's floor tiles were mass-produced to a single identical design. Every toilet seat in Israel came from one kibbutz factory. Foreign investors who wanted to set up shop were required to sell a 51% stake in their companies to the Histadrut, Israel's trade union congress, which functioned less as a labour movement than as a parallel state. This was a society in which the collective was paramount and the individual was expected to subordinate private ambition to the national project.

That world has vanished. The kibbutzim have privatized, their members now owning their own homes and earning different wages according to their work rather than receiving an equal share regardless of contribution. But the kibbutz is merely a metaphor for what has happened to wider Israeli society. The country has privatized with a vengeance, its people withdrawing from the collective sphere into a sphere of personal ambition, consumer choice and individual comfort. Israelis have a word for the place they now inhabit: the "bu'ah," or bubble. It is the mental and emotional enclosure they construct to hide from the anxiety of the country's security situation, the relentless grind of conflict and the ever-present threat of violence. The polling data captures the dichotomy with startling clarity: nearly 40% of Israelis believe their country faces a serious threat of destruction, yet 83% say they are satisfied with their own personal lives. They are, in other words, content to live inside the bubble, peering out at the dangers beyond but reluctant to leave the comfort within.

It is this complacency that casts its shadow over the 60th anniversary. The US administration has set a political deadline for progress in the Annapolis peace talks, ahead of President George W. Bush's visit to the region. Israel insists it is striving for peace. Foreign Minister Tzipi Livni takes pride in the fact that she has continued talking to the Palestinians even on what she calls "days of terror." Prime Minister Ehud Olmert's hawkish critics, meanwhile, charge that his peace efforts are merely a distraction from the corruption inquiry that hangs over him like a storm cloud. But for all the talk, there is no genuine Israeli urgency for an accord. When Jimmy Carter visited Jerusalem carrying messages from Hamas and Damascus, no frontline Israeli minister would meet him. The official explanation was that Israel cannot legitimize Hamas for fear of undermining Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas. Yet Olmert has done nothing tangible to bolster Abbas — no removal of illegal West Bank outposts, no compensation packages to encourage settlers to leave. The contradiction is telling: Israel claims to be protecting Abbas, but its actions suggest it is content to let him wither.

The cynical national mood is not hard to fathom. Israelis point to the withdrawals from Lebanon in 2000 and Gaza in 2005 and ask what they got in return: Katyushas from Hezbollah in the north, Qassams from Hamas in the south. The lesson many have drawn is that territorial compromise does not bring peace but merely relocates the threat. Meanwhile, the separation barrier has largely contained terrorism inside the Palestinian territories, and the "demographic threat" — the prospect of Arabs eventually outnumbering Jews in the territory under Israeli control — feels too abstract, too distant, to force action. Israelis believe they can survive without peace, and on the evidence of their daily lives, they may be right. But survival is not the same as security, and stasis is not the same as stability.

So the anniversary will be celebrated with the confidence of a country whose economy grew at 5.1% last year, whose hi-tech sector is the envy of the region, whose citizens are, by their own account, happy with their lives. The street parties will be loud and the beach barbecues will be well attended. But somewhere, in the small hours, a few insomniacs will be watching those old reruns — the kibbutz choirs in their work shirts and peasant skirts, singing the songs of a collective dream that has faded. And if they stay tuned long enough, they might hear a melody that sounds more passé now than ever: "Shir L'shalom," the song for peace. It was once the anthem of a movement. Tonight it sounds like a ghost.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_22
body:
Apologies are easier to give than to receive. That is one of the first lessons of domestic life, learned at the kitchen table or in the hallway after some sharp exchange. The one who says sorry seizes the moral high ground; they have performed the noble act, made the gracious gesture. The recipient, by contrast, is trapped. If they accept, they are somehow diminished, their grievance washed away in a tide of reconciliation. If they refuse, they are damned as obstinate, bitter, unable to move on. The apology, supposedly an act of humility, is often an exercise in power. Scale this up from the kitchen table to Northern Ireland, and you get some sense of the strange position in which the province now finds itself.

For the IRA's statement this week was, by any measure, a remarkable document. It spoke the language of psychotherapy rather than politics, of "hurt," "pain" and "grief." It expressed sorrow, offered condolences, acknowledged suffering. And it did so without the customary republican reflex of blaming the British government for everything. There was no precondition, no "but," no equivocation. The focus was narrow but pointed: the IRA was sorry for the deaths of "non-combatants." The timing was not accidental. The statement was prompted by the approaching anniversary of Bloody Friday, the 1972 bombing spree that killed nine people and left a lasting scar on Belfast. That the IRA chose to address its own atrocities, rather than those perpetrated against it, was itself a kind of progress.

The first reactions told their own story. They were split, and split along predictable lines. Some unionists were moved. Tom Donnelly, whose sister died on Bloody Friday, spoke with a quiet dignity that was hard to dismiss. He was hopeful, he said. He believed the statement was sincere. If a man who had lost a sister to IRA bombs could find it in himself to welcome such a gesture, who were the rest of us to sneer? But most unionists were not in Donnelly's camp. They were cynical, dismissing the apology as "pure PR," a stunt timed for maximum political effect. And they had reason to be suspicious, because the political stakes could hardly have been higher.

For the following Wednesday, the British government must declare whether the IRA is honouring its ceasefire. If the verdict is negative, David Trimble will demand Sinn Féin's expulsion from the Northern Ireland executive, and the peace process will lurch into yet another crisis. The timing of the apology, coming just days before that deadline, was either a happy coincidence or a calculated manoeuvre. Most unionists assumed the latter.

And who can blame them? Unionist faith in the peace process has collapsed. The 1998 Good Friday Agreement was always seen by many in the unionist community as a moral outrage, a deal that released convicted terrorists from prison and put Sinn Féin into government while the IRA kept its guns. It was tolerated only in the hope that republicans would leave violence behind. That hope has been destroyed. The Colombia arrests, in which three republicans were found with FARC guerrillas, suggested the IRA was still in the business of exporting expertise in explosives. The Castlereagh break-in, in which sensitive security files were stolen from a police station, pointed to ongoing IRA intelligence-gathering. And the nightly street violence in Belfast, with loyalist and republican mobs clashing in sectarian confrontations, made a mockery of the word "peace." If assembly elections were held now, Ian Paisley's anti-agreement Democratic Unionists would overwhelm Trimble's Ulster Unionists. The centre is not holding.

What unionists want is a US envoy, appointed by George W Bush, to assess ceasefire compliance. They believe such a figure would bring independent scrutiny to bear on republican activity, reassure their base that someone is watching, and buy Trimble the time he needs to hold his party together. It is not an unreasonable demand. But republicans reject it with jesuitical rigour. Anything not explicitly in the Good Friday Agreement, they insist, is a "widening" of the ceasefire terms, an attempt to impose new conditions retroactively. They dismiss the envoy idea and any suggestion of an expanded definition of ceasefire compliance. They expect Tony Blair to deliver a verdict ambiguous enough to keep Sinn Féin in government and the process on track. And they may well be right to expect it. The timing between the IRA statement, London's response and Dublin's reaction had a choreographed quality that was hard to miss. This was a dance, and the steps had been rehearsed.

But let us be fair. For the IRA to issue such a statement was a big deal. This is a self-styled liberation movement that believes its cause was just, that its war was legitimate, that its volunteers were soldiers, not murderers. To apologise for any of it is to concede ground that such movements do not give up lightly. Recall how recently Sinn Féin could barely bring itself to express regret for anything. Now the IRA itself has apologised, using language that would have been unthinkable a decade ago. That is not nothing.

Try a thought experiment. Project yourself to September 2031. Imagine al-Qaida's ruling council issuing a statement of regret for 9/11, thirty years on. Would it heal the victims' families? Probably not. The wound would be too deep, the loss too great. But if al-Qaida had been on ceasefire for six or eight years, wouldn't the world feel relieved by such a statement? Wouldn't it signal something about intent, about direction of travel, about the organisation's understanding that the past cannot be undone but the future can be different? Wouldn't it matter?

The IRA's apology has its limits, of course. It should have included "combatants" too, the RUC officers and British soldiers who were killed in the line of duty. To exclude them is to maintain the fiction that the IRA's war was somehow cleaner, more discriminating, than it was. It was not. The distinction between combatant and non-combatant casualties is one the IRA has drawn for its own comfort, not for moral clarity.

But ask yourself this: what sinister purpose were these "ploys" actually serving? The answer is only to keep republicans in government, as the agreement allows. Decommissioning, the ceasefire, the apology itself, they all serve one end: keeping the peace process afloat now that republicans have decided it serves their strategic interest. If that is cynicism, it is cynicism in the service of something better than the alternative.

What we are watching is republicanism on a slow, incomplete journey from paramilitary past to political future. It is not neat or perfect. But it is more real with each passing month. And in peace processes, as in life, sometimes you must know how to say yes.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_25
body:
"If it ain't broke, don't fix it." That has been the conservative refrain for as long as anyone can remember — the instinctive defence of Britain's unwritten constitution, muttered whenever anyone dared suggest that perhaps the patchwork of custom, convention, precedent and occasional statute might benefit from a little systematic tidying. The argument has always had a certain seductive simplicity. It has worked for centuries, this higgledy-piggledy arrangement, so why meddle? The Americans may have their pristine parchment, the Germans their Grundgesetz, the French their Fifth Republic codified in black and white, but we British muddle through — and muddling through, we are told, is a kind of genius.

Except that it isn't working any more. The system is broke. And the evidence is not hard to find.

The true test of any constitution is not its elegance or its antiquity but the quality of governance it produces. By that measure, Britain's constitutional arrangement has been failing for the better part of two decades — and the failures are not isolated mishaps but systemic ones, rooted in a structure of government that lacks the checks, balances and scrutiny mechanisms to prevent calamity or to hold those responsible properly to account.

Consider the catalogue. The pension mis-selling scandal of the late 1980s and early 1990s, in which millions of people were persuaded out of secure occupational schemes into worthless private plans — a disaster that unfolded under the nose of regulators who either could not or would not act. The BSE crisis, which saw government assurances that British beef was safe dissolve into the admission that mad cow disease had been spreading while ministers parroted the line that there was no evidence of risk to humans. The epidemic of parliamentary sleaze that discredited the Commons in the mid-1990s, as MPs were caught taking cash for questions and accepting paid advocacy from companies whose interests they were supposed to scrutinise. The botched privatisation of the railways, which handed over a public asset to private operators while leaving no one — no minister, no regulator, no executive — clearly accountable when things went wrong. The poll tax, a fiscal atrocity imposed by a government that had lost any meaningful connection to popular consent. And then, crowning the lot, the Millennium Dome — that billion-pound monument to ministerial vanity, a project nobody wanted, nobody needed, and nobody could stop, because the constitutional system provided no mechanism by which such a thing could be halted once a prime minister had set his heart on it.

These were not merely individual errors of judgement, the kind of mistakes any government might make. They were structural failures — products of a system in which executive power is effectively unconstrained, in which scrutiny is weak, in which the governing party controls the legislature, and in which the absence of any entrenched constitutional safeguards means there is no brake, no filter, no second opinion between a prime minister's whim and national policy. The Scott inquiry into the arms-for-Iraq affair confirmed as much, finding that ministers had bent the rules on arms exports to Iraq not through individual malice but through a system that allowed them to evade parliamentary accountability almost at will. The Phillips report on BSE said the same thing in a different register: the failure was not primarily one of individuals but of the system of governance itself — the way information flowed, the way decisions were made, the way dissent was stifled. When two such authoritative inquiries tell you the same thing, it is no longer possible to dismiss the problem as a run of bad luck. The flaw is in the machinery.

And the public knows it. Confidence in the institutions of British government has been collapsing for the better part of a generation. The evidence is in the polling data, and it makes grim reading. According to surveys tracking public trust, confidence in the House of Commons fell by roughly half between 1985 and 1995 — a steeper decline than in virtually any other western democracy. A 1996 EU poll ranked Britons' faith in their national parliament near the bottom of all member states, below the Belgians and only just above the Italians, whose political system was at the time dissolving in a corruption scandal of operatic proportions. Local election turnout has been sliding for years, with fewer and fewer citizens bothering to exercise even the minimal civic function of casting a ballot for their council. A Mori survey found that 71% of first-time voters believed their vote would make little or no difference to how the country was governed. And in a Leeds by-election, turnout fell below 20% — a figure that would shame a banana republic. People are voting with their feet, and what they are saying is that the system does not listen, does not deliver, and does not deserve their participation.

What makes this moment different — what makes the case for a new constitutional settlement urgent rather than merely desirable — is that the old conservative defence has already been demolished, and by the Conservatives' own successors in government. Since 1997, Labour has undertaken a programme of constitutional change so radical that historians have to reach back to the 1830s for a comparable upheaval. Devolution to Scotland and Wales, the restoration of a parliament in Northern Ireland, the incorporation of the European Convention on Human Rights into British law, a freedom of information act, partial reform of the House of Lords — each one of these would once have been considered a seismic constitutional event. Together, they amount to the most sweeping restructuring of the British state in 170 years.

The conservative myth of an untouchable, mystical constitution — a sacred inheritance too delicate to be handled by modern hands — has been shattered. Labour has already tinkered, and tinkered deeply. The patchwork quilt is already being unstitched. Which means the question is no longer whether to reform the constitution but whether to do it properly — or leave it in a dangerous, half-finished state that satisfies neither the old logic nor the new.

**Four Nations**

Devolution has forced Britain to confront a question it spent two centuries avoiding: what kind of country is it? For generations, Britain acted as a unitary state, governed from Westminster, with Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland treated as regions with peculiar cultural habits but no distinct political identity. Devolution has exposed that as a fiction. Britain is not a unitary state at all but a nascent, stumbling, half-conscious multinational entity — a union of four nations, each with its own identity and, increasingly, its own institutions.

But Labour's decentralisation has opened questions without resolving them. What about England? Should Westminster continue to serve as a federal legislature, dealing with UK-wide matters, while doubling — awkwardly — as an English parliament for domestic business? Why should Scotland enjoy its own parliament while England, with ten times the population, has none? And then there is the West Lothian question, which Tam Dalyell posed a quarter of a century ago and which devolution has transformed from a theoretical puzzle into a live political grievance: why should Scottish MPs vote on English health and education policy when English MPs have no say over Scottish equivalents, those matters having been devolved to Edinburgh? The worms were always in the can. Now the can is open, and they are wriggling out in every direction.

**Shattered Mystique**

Nowhere is the unfinished nature of Labour's project more visible than in the House of Lords. The removal of most hereditary peers was meant to solve the second chamber's problems — to drag it into the democratic age, stripping away the anachronism of legislators sitting by virtue of birth. Instead, it has exposed the democratic deficit in the starkest possible terms.

When the hereditaries dominated the Lords, the chamber could be dismissed as a Ruritanian joke — a museum piece, a relic, a constitutional oddity that no one took too seriously because no one was meant to. But now, with the hereditaries gone, Britons look at a supposedly reformed second chamber in which not a single member is elected. The old disguise has been stripped away, and what is revealed is a body of appointees — chosen by the prime minister, answerable to no one, accountable to nobody. The democratic deficit is not hidden; it is on display.

The old Tory position — leave well alone, don't tinker, the thing works in its own mysterious way — is dead. Once you start to reform, you cannot stop halfway and hope for the best. You have to do it properly, and that means a second chamber that is either elected or chosen by a method that confers genuine democratic legitimacy. The current hybrid of ancient custom and New Labour modernisation risks being the worst of both worlds — stripped of the mystique that once protected it, but lacking the democratic legitimacy that would justify its existence. Even William Hague and the Conservatives have begun flirting with radical constitutional ideas — an English parliament, a federal Britain — that would once have been anathema to them. The ground is shifting under everyone's feet.

And it is shifting not just at home but abroad. Britain's constitutional crisis coincides with a period of profound external upheaval, one that raises the most fundamental questions about sovereignty and self-government. The upcoming EU summit in Nice is debating the future shape of the European Union — whether to extend majority voting, how to handle a common currency and shared military force, whether to draw up a basic law for Europe that would define the powers of the union against those of its member states. These are constitutional questions of the highest order, and Britain is approaching them with its own internal constitution in a state of confusion and disrepair.

Widen the lens and the picture grows more dramatic still. The anti-capitalist protests that erupted in Seattle and then Prague have posed, in their own chaotic and sometimes incoherent way, the most basic political question of all: who rules the world? Is it the people, through their governments, or is it corporations, the World Trade Organisation and the International Monetary Fund — forces that no citizen voted for and no government seems able to control? In every arena, from the global to the local, the debate turns on the same axis: sovereignty. Who should be in charge? Who has the right to decide? This is the question for the world, for Europe — and for Britain.

Three pressures are now converging on the British state. The old system is failing, producing governance that is incompetent, unaccountable and increasingly illegitimate in the eyes of its citizens. Labour's reforms have changed the landscape beyond recognition but have left the job half-done, creating contradictions and anomalies that demand resolution. And the external environment — Europe, globalisation, the sovereignty debate — is pressing in, demanding that Britain know what it is and what it wants before it can negotiate what it will be.

These three pressures point in the same direction: toward a new, written constitutional settlement. Such a settlement would do three things. It would improve governance, by building in the checks, balances and mechanisms of accountability that the current system conspicuously lacks. It would complete Labour's unfinished business, resolving the contradictions of devolution, Lords reform and human rights incorporation by embedding them in a coherent whole. And it would help prevent the union from unravelling, by defining the relationships between the four nations in a way that is fair, transparent and democratically defensible — before resentment and grievance do the job instead, messily and destructively.

Britain cannot hope to clarify its relationship with Europe or the wider world while it remains confused about its own internal sovereignty. It cannot lecture others on democratic governance while operating a system that allows a prime minister to make war, sign treaties and appoint judges through the exercise of powers that belong to the crown — powers that are, in practice, exercised by one person without parliamentary consent. And it cannot expect its citizens to re-engage with politics while the structures of politics themselves remain opaque, unaccountable and resistant to the most basic democratic principles.

The moth-eaten garb of the past no longer fits. A new century and a new millennium demand a new constitution — written down, democratically endorsed, and owned by the people it governs. Not muddling through, but knowing where you stand. Not the accumulated wisdom of centuries, but the deliberate choice of citizens. It is time.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_27
body:
It is the season for performance reviews. Schools, hospitals, corporations — all of them submit to the annual ritual of appraisal, asking where they went right and where they went astray. Columnists, who are happy enough to dish out judgment the rest of the year, should be no exception. And so, in a neat bit of symmetry, why not end the year as we began it? On January 1, this column made six reckless predictions for the year now ending. It is time to see how many hit the mark — and how many missed badly.

**1. The judicial inquiry.** Prediction first. I said the judge would steer clear of the ground most perilous to the prime minister, declining to deliver a killer sentence accusing him of leading the country falsely to war. I reckoned that, as an unelected judge, he would tremble at the thought of toppling an elected head of government. Instead, I forecast, he would spread the pain evenly: the BBC faulted for sloppy editorial procedures, the government criticised for its naming strategy, the prime minister biting his lower lip and surviving. Half right, half wrong — and the half I got wrong was the half that mattered. The prime minister did survive; the BBC was indeed faulted. But I had imagined a judge who would sprinkle blame like confetti, ensuring no one walked away undamaged. Instead, his lordship inflicted damage on only one side, and ruled more boldly than I had anticipated on the central controversy. The pain was not spread evenly; it was aimed. I had expected judicial caution and got judicial nerve. That I did not see coming.

**2. The US election.** I called the winner right — George W Bush walked it — but I badly misidentified his opponent. I had backed Howard Dean to dominate the early contests, sweep through South Carolina and emerge as the nominee. Instead, Dean's campaign imploded with a scream in Iowa that will echo through political trivia quizzes for decades. The man who actually faced Bush, John Kerry, was someone I had effectively written off. The lesson here is one I should have learned long ago: never trust the conventional wisdom of political insiders, the pundits and operatives who insisted Dean was inevitable. They were wrong, and I was wrong to listen to them.

**3. Iraq.** I rightly predicted that day-to-day anti-occupation violence would continue, taking the shine off the capture of Saddam Hussein. I dismissed the optimistic talk that followed the dictator's arrest — the notion that he had been directing the resistance from his spider hole — and on that score I was correct. But I wrongly anticipated that the Americans, mindful of their electoral timetable, would start looking for exits: negotiating a deal with the French, scheduling elections, bringing troops home to televised homecomings. None of that happened. The occupation ground on, bloodier than before, with no exit strategy and no timetable. I had assumed domestic political pressure would force a withdrawal. I underestimated the capacity of an election-year administration to absorb bad news from abroad and simply keep going.

**4. Israel and Palestine.** I correctly sensed that Ariel Sharon's old approach would come to be seen as futile — that a consensus would emerge, from the military elites down, that his way was getting nowhere. What I did not foresee was that Sharon himself would be the one to say so. I expected pressure to build around him; instead, he initiated his own dramatic departure from the Gaza strip, a unilateral disengagement that stunned friend and foe alike. I also wrongly predicted that corruption allegations would engulf him and force his resignation. They didn't. Once again, a leader facing clear evidence of wrongdoing simply carried on. The pattern was becoming familiar.

**5. The home front.** I predicted the opposition leader would have a solid year, bolstered by a decent result in June's European elections. In the narrowest, most technical sense, this came true: Michael Howard's Conservatives did top the nationwide poll. But the vote share was historically dismal — barely above the levels that had doomed his predecessor. Topping a poll with 33% is like winning a race in which nobody broke into a jog. Howard had a year, all right, but it was the kind of year that prompts talk of "consolidation" rather than celebration.

**6. Domestic figures.** Two hits here, and they offer some consolation. I correctly predicted that Ken Livingstone would be readmitted to the Labour party and re-elected as mayor of London, and that Gordon Brown would stay on as chancellor — and wait. Both came to pass with the inevitability of a Greek tragedy, though one performed at the pace of a particularly slow-moving farise. Brown waited; Livingstone returned. Sometimes the obvious prediction is the best one.

So what lessons does this little performance review yield? Two, and they cut against each other in instructive ways.

The first is that I should have distrusted the conventional wisdom of political insiders. My biggest single error — picking Dean as the Democratic nominee — came from listening to the buzz of the consultant class, the cable television sages and the blogosphere's self-appointed electability experts. They were all talking to each other, reinforcing each other's assumptions, and I mistook their echo chamber for the sound of the real world. The lesson is clear: when the insiders all agree, that is precisely the moment to look elsewhere.

The second lesson is more surprising, perhaps even embarrassing, for a journalist to admit. I was insufficiently cynical. I expected institutions of accountability to function fairly and predictably — judges to spread blame evenly, corruption scandals to force resignations, electoral timetables to drive policy. None of that happened. Inquiries that should have been even-handed turned out to be aimed. Scandals that should have been fatal turned out to be survivable. Political pressure that should have produced change simply dissipated. I had assumed the mechanisms of accountability would work as advertised. They did not.

And this points to the broader theme that defined the year: unaccountability. Again and again, powerful figures survived despite clear evidence of failure or wrongdoing. The prime minister weathered the inquiry. Sharon shrugged off the corruption allegations. The occupation continued without an exit plan. No senior figure paid a price for any of it. Heads that should have rolled stayed firmly on shoulders. The year will be remembered as the one in which accountability itself went missing — not through any grand conspiracy, but through the slow, grinding accretion of impunity. Leaders learned that they could weather storms simply by refusing to acknowledge the rain.

So the year taught me two lessons, and I intend to hold them close. First, don't expect accountability mechanisms to work — not because they are broken, but because they are not really meant to. They are theatre, and I kept mistaking the stage for the world. Second, don't make predictions. The world is too turbulent, too contrary, too rigged against the forecaster's art. Who, after all, could have predicted that a cabinet minister would be caught in a personal scandal involving a tryst with his married secretary in a Sheffield flat — discovered not by investigative journalists but by the secretary's husband, who had returned home early to find the minister's car in the driveway and his dog locked in the kitchen? You couldn't make it up. And I, for one, will stop trying.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_31
body:
The guardians of civic probity have spoken, and all is well. First there was the cash-for-passports inquiry, which examined the case of billionaire donors whose applications for British citizenship were personally championed by a senior cabinet minister. After due deliberation, the inquiry found nothing "improper." Then came the parliamentary sleaze committee, which investigated a minister accused of multiple breaches and cleared him of nearly all charges—while declining to punish the one minor violation it did find. And who could forget the Downing Street intervention in a planning application lodged by a billionaire arms broker? That was dismissed as mere concern over "timing." Three cases, three clean bills of health. The system worked. The rules were followed. Nothing to see here.

Except that it doesn't feel right, does it? If the rules say nothing wrong happened, why doesn't it feel right? That is the question that refuses to go away, nagging at the edge of public life like a toothache that no amount of aspirin will dull. We are told that procedures were followed, that guidelines were respected, that the proper authorities have examined the matter and found it wanting in nothing. And yet the public reads these exoneration reports and experiences not relief but something closer to nausea. The gap between what the rules call "proper" and what the public recognises as just is widening into a chasm. The question is what, if anything, can be done about it.

Forget the inquiries. Strip away the procedural language and the legalistic hedging, and these are all versions of the same story. Someone with money got special treatment. Take the passport case. Even the exoneration report itself documents, in painstaking detail, that the billionaire's application was personally championed by the head of the immigration directorate. It records that residency rules were bent to accelerate processing. It notes that a roster of former prime ministers and senior ministers lobbied on the applicants' behalf. The report calls all of this "proper." The public knows what it really is. It is the exercise of influence by the wealthy to obtain a result that would never have been available to anyone who lacked either the money or the connections—or both.

The pattern repeats with dreary regularity. There was the Formula One boss whose large donation to the governing party was followed, with remarkable promptness, by an exemption from the tobacco advertising ban that threatened his sport's finances. There was the party treasurer whose massive donations were followed, in due course, by a peerage. In each case, the official machinery whirred into action and produced a verdict of innocence. In each case, the public drew a different conclusion. The officials' definition of "proper" is itself the problem. We need a new one.

Consider, then, the standard remedies. First, there is state funding of political parties. This would free politicians from groveling to millionaires, severing the link between donation and favour at a stroke. It sounds clean and logical. But it wouldn't have prevented the passport case, because the billionaires in question were donating to a cultural project—the Millennium Dome—not to a party. The corruption, such as it was, operated through a different channel entirely. State funding of parties leaves that channel wide open. And it carries its own risks: starving new political movements of resources, entrenching the established parties, and requiring taxpayers to subsidise politicians they despise.

Second, there is better regulation by parliamentary committees. But the committee that just investigated the minister had to drop most of its charges because he refused to cooperate. They were powerless. He simply stonewalled, and they had no mechanism to force his hand. The regulatory route is structurally inadequate: it relies on the willingness of the powerful to submit to scrutiny, and when they decline, the whole apparatus grinds to a halt. A watchdog that cannot bite is worse than no watchdog at all, because it creates the illusion of oversight while providing none.

There is, however, a more radical remedy, drawn from the law. The core offense in all these cases is the same: a violation of the principle that everyone should be equal under the law. There is one rule for the rich and another for everyone else. This is not merely a slogan. It is a legal principle with deep roots and real force.

Consider the United States. The 14th Amendment to the American constitution, ratified in 1868, contains a clause promising "equal protection of the laws." That clause has underpinned civil rights litigation from Brown v. Board of Education in 1954 onward, allowing citizens to petition courts to outlaw preferential treatment for one group over another. It has been the engine of some of the most profound social change in American history—not because politicians willed it, but because citizens used the courts to force their hand.

Britain now has its own analog. Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights has been absorbed into domestic law through the Human Rights Act. That article bars discrimination on grounds of sex, race, colour, and "any other status." And "other status" could encompass wealth. The implications are potentially enormous. Imagine two ordinary citizens petitioning the courts to ask why their passport application wasn't handled as swiftly as a billionaire's. Imagine a small developer complaining that his planning decision wasn't expedited like a billionaire's.
