Imagine a modest business owner claiming he was denied a policy exemption granted to a wealthy donor.

Keir Starmer, a leading human rights lawyer and former director of public prosecutions, confirms that discrimination against those with less money could fall within Article 14's prohibitions. The key requirement, he explains, is that the claimant could amass evidence and prove the cases were genuinely comparable in every respect bar wealth. That is a high bar—but not an impossible one. And at minimum, such a challenge would force the authority to explain and justify its differential treatment. It would compel them to stand up in open court and say, for the record, why the billionaire got the fast track and the ordinary citizen did not. That alone would represent a seismic shift in the balance of power.

Experts always said the Human Rights Act would make a big difference. In the United States, the Bill of Rights has long been used to advance civil rights causes that politicians found too politically difficult to touch. Now it is Britain's turn. For those sick of fast tracks and special favors for the rich, the political system is too compromised to be the solution. Politicians will not regulate themselves out of a game that serves them so well. The committees will not enforce rules they lack the power to enforce. The inquiries will continue to find nothing improper, because the definition of improper is the thing that needs changing. It is time to go to court.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_132
body:
When I arrived in Washington as a correspondent nearly two decades ago, the late Alistair Cooke, then still dispensing his weekly Letter from America with undimmed authority, gave me a piece of advice I have never forgotten. The hardest thing for British people to grasp about the American political system, he said, is not the federal structure, or the separation of powers, or even the primary system. It is how important judges and courts are within it. Americans do not see the judiciary as a separate branch of government in the way we do — as a sort of constitutional backstop, brought out for ceremonial occasions and otherwise left to get on with things quietly. They see it as the third co-equal branch, fully as central to the governance of the nation as the presidency or the Congress.

Cooke's advice came back to me this week for two reasons. The first was that John McCain was in London delivering the inaugural Alistair Cooke memorial lecture — an event that inevitably prompted reflection on Cooke's long, observant life on the interface between the two cultures. The second was that George Bush was crossing the Atlantic for the G8 summit at Gleneagles, where the agenda was supposed to be dominated by global poverty and climate change, with Iraq hovering in the background as always. Yet the topic that was consuming Washington had nothing to do with any of these things. It was, instead, the resignation of a single 75-year-old woman from a job that most British people could not name, still less explain.

Over the Fourth of July weekend, Justice Sandra Day O'Connor announced her retirement from the United States Supreme Court. This was not merely a major Washington story. It was a potentially seismic national political event — at least as important, in its own context, as the election of a president or a new Congress. The structural centrality of the nine-justice court to American government is something that British people, weaned on the fiction of parliamentary sovereignty, find almost impossible to absorb. The court rules on the constitutionality of legislation, on the boundaries of federal and state power, on the most intimate questions of individual liberty. It has been 11 years since the last nomination to the court, an unusually long gap. And O'Connor herself was no ordinary justice. For the better part of a decade and a half, she had been the swing voter on a closely divided bench, shaping the lives of millions of Americans on questions of abortion, race, religion and the proper reach of government. Her replacement could shift the balance of the court for a generation.

The right sees this as an immense opportunity. The left sees it as a moment of direst threat. It is an open secret in Washington that the Republican lobbies have long prioritised reshaping the court in the aggressively conservative mould of Justice Antonin Scalia, with a view to dismantling the role of the federal government that has stood since at least the New Deal. Bush, with no election to face, may well share this ambition. Yet there are countervailing constraints. Most Americans are decent and pragmatic people, offended by the excesses of the right's culture wars — as opinion polls consistently show and as the unedifying Terry Schiavo affair recently reminded them. Republican candidates facing mid-term elections next year may be uneasy about the electoral consequences of nominating an outright culture-warrior to the bench. And Bush, whatever his instincts, has a well-established pattern of not ignoring the political middle ground — the territory where O'Connor's cautious pragmatism played so well. He will also face pressure to nominate another woman.

All of this makes for a process that is messy, public, expensive and deeply politicised. Now consider the British contrast. Here is a general knowledge question. Who is Britain's newly appointed lord chief justice, taking over from Lord Woolf on October 1? The answer is Nicholas Phillips, Lord Phillips of Worth Matravers, who has been presiding over the court of appeal for the past five years. Alongside him, Sir Anthony Clarke becomes master of the rolls, and Sir Igor Judge — surely the best name on the English bench — becomes head of the Queen's Bench Division. Most readers, I suspect, will not have known any of this. Nor would they have learned it easily. These three major appointments were announced quietly in a press release containing almost no information about the appointees. They received only cursory media attention. They appeared on no front pages. They prompted no follow-up coverage. And they generated no public debate about whether these were the right people, where they stood on significant legal issues, or where these appointments might take the English courts in the years ahead.

This is striking and eloquent in itself. But it is all the more striking given that these were the first appointments under the major changes brought in by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005 — changes that were supposed to mark a new era of transparency and accountability in the relationship between the judiciary, the government and the legislature. It might have been expected that such landmark appointments would be marked in a way that signalled to the public that these are key figures in a new constitutional era — that the lord chief justice and his senior colleagues matter not just to lawyers but to citizens. In the event, nothing of the sort occurred. The most senior appointments in the supposedly reformed judicial system were made as they always have been — unheralded, unexamined and with minimal openness.

Back in America, it is reported that as much as fifty million dollars may be spent in the battle for O'Connor's seat. Two other justices are in their 80s and a third is a cancer sufferer. The process may soon be repeated with greater intensity, greater expense and still more epochal consequences. From a distance, this can seem a travesty, even a corruption, of a process for selecting senior members of an independent, merit-based judiciary. The spectacle of well-funded lobby groups on left and right mobilising to influence the composition of the bench is not an edifying one. It subordinates legal principle to political calculation in ways that many Americans themselves find disturbing.

Yet the American process, for all its faults, at least explicitly and implicitly recognises the centrality of judges and the law to the constitutional processes of modern democratic government. It acknowledges that who sits on the supreme court matters to everyone, not just to lawyers. It treats the question as one worthy of national attention, national debate and national engagement. By contrast, Britain — with rare and distinguished exceptions such as Lord Woolf himself — remains overwhelmingly in denial about these connections. We continue to behave as though the judiciary is a self-regulating profession whose most senior appointments are nobody's business but its own. We treat the naming of those who will shape our laws and our liberties as a matter of administrative routine, not constitutional significance.

If citizens meekly concede that they have no right to know about the highest judges in the land, or to call their thinking to public account, they vacate an important part of the public arena. Unless we can respect judges, it is harder to respect the law. And unless we respect the law, it is harder to respect the system of government of which both law and judges are integral parts. America's process is imperfect. But it is built on a recognition that ours still lacks.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_138
body:
A few weeks ago, I found myself in conversation with an American political historian of moderately conservative instincts. We were talking, as one does in election years, about the transformation of the Republican party. At one point, my companion observed that the GOP had once been a party of two wings. There was the grand wing — the progressive, "compassionate conservative" tradition of Theodore Roosevelt and, later, Nelson Rockefeller — which believed that government had a role to play in tempering the excesses of capitalism and in lifting up the disadvantaged. And there was the more modest wing — the practical, businesslike, essentially cautious tradition of Dwight Eisenhower and Richard Nixon — which was sceptical of grand schemes but accepted the broad postwar consensus. Neither wing was radical. Neither was driven by rage. Both assumed that politics was the art of the possible.

That Republican party is gone. What has replaced it is something altogether different: an alliance between a dominant, aggressive, religiously driven activist base and a moderate electorate that persists in the touching belief that the party it votes for is essentially benign. The activists know what they want. The moderates do not seem to realise what is being done in their name. The 2004 Republican convention in New York was the most vivid demonstration of this new dispensation.

The hostility on display at Madison Square Garden was not spontaneous. It was orchestrated. The personal attacks on John Kerry were relentless and systematic. Delegates sported Purple Heart band-aids on their cheeks, mocking Kerry's Vietnam war wounds as if decorated military service were something to be ridiculed rather than respected. The platform speeches were brutal. Georgia Senator Zell Miller, a Democrat turned Republican attack dog, delivered the convention's most venomous address, characterising Kerry as "wrong, weak and wobbly" — a man who would defend America with "spitballs." The hall loved it.

What made this spectacle so striking was the identity of the men on whose behalf it was staged. George W Bush spent the Vietnam war in the Texas Air National Guard, safely stateside. Dick Cheney, when asked about his own non-service, icily observed that he "had other priorities" during the Vietnam years. Neither man saw combat. Neither man went anywhere near it. Yet it was these two non-combatants who presided over a convention whose central theme was that Kerry — a man who had actually been shot at in the Mekong Delta — was insufficiently martial to be entrusted with the nation's defence.

The hypocrisy is so flagrant as to seem almost beside the point. But it is the point. The GOP's goal is not reverence for military service. It is exclusive ownership of defence and security politics. The party has calculated that if it can monopolise the imagery and language of national security, it need not actually practise what it preaches. Bush and Cheney can be hawks without having been soldiers, because the cause is not military valour but political dominance. Kerry's actual combat record is not an asset to be honoured but an obstacle to be neutralised, mocked and diminished.

What was equally remarkable about the convention was the total absence of any criticism of the military. Not a word was breathed about Abu Ghraib. Not a syllable suggested that anything done by anyone in uniform might warrant scrutiny. The military, in the GOP's new dispensation, is beyond critique. It is a sacred instrument, to be invoked but never questioned. This is not the attitude of a party that takes the military seriously. It is the attitude of a party that has decided the military is too useful politically to be subjected to democratic accountability.

It is worth pausing here to consider a counterfactual. If Al Gore had been president on September 11 2001, does anyone seriously believe the Republican party would have rallied around him? Does anyone imagine they would have suspended partisanship in the national interest? Of course not. They would have blamed him. Indeed, they would have blamed Bill Clinton, just as they have spent years trying to do. Walk into any Barnes and Noble in New York and you will find entire shelves devoted to the proposition that Clinton was responsible for 9/11. Books with titles like "Losing Bin Laden," "Dereliction of Duty," "Intelligence Failure" — all arguing, with varying degrees of sophistication and honesty, that the catastrophe was the fault of the previous Democratic administration. The Republican party does not do bipartisan grace in defeat. It does vengeance.

Which is why a President Kerry would face something unprecedented in modern American politics: an opposition party whose reflexive response to any setback would be impeachment. Not loyal opposition. Not constructive criticism. Vindictive, relentless, personalised opposition of the kind that was directed at Clinton throughout his presidency and that would be directed at Kerry from his first day in office. The Republican party has developed an impeachment reflex. It is part of its DNA now. Kerry would not be a president to be opposed. He would be a president to be destroyed.

In many ways, the modern GOP is the political expression of something Eisenhower warned about in his farewell address in 1961. He spoke of the "unwarranted influence" and "misplaced power" of the military-industrial complex. He warned that public policy could become "the captive of a scientific-technological elite." The party that Eisenhower once led has now become the vehicle for precisely the forces he feared. It is a militarist party, fuelled by testosterone-driven chants of "U-S-A, U-S-A" — a party that measures patriotism by decibel level and equates dissent with treason.

But the militarism is only half the story. The other half is the fusion of military-industrial power with religious imperatives. This is where Bush's political technique becomes especially important. Bush is a master of what might be called "double-coding" — using language that sounds moderate and inclusive to the untrained ear but that signals specific commitments to the religious right. When Bush speaks of "culture of life," he is not making a philosophical observation. He is signalling opposition to abortion. When he speaks of "the sanctity of marriage," he is not reflecting on sociology. He is signalling opposition to gay rights. The moderate electorate hears reassurance. The activist base hears mobilisation.

At a private rally during the convention week, Kansas Senator Sam Brownback declared that America was engaged in a "culture war" — language that would have been inconceivable at a Republican convention in the era of Eisenhower or even Reagan. The convention's closing prayer asked for divine guidance against "the forces of evil" — a phrase that, in the context of an election campaign, can only mean the Democratic party and everything it represents. This is not the language of democratic politics. It is the language of crusade.

Much of what was on display in New York was, in the most precise sense of the word, Orwellian. War was peace. Aggression was defence. Dissent was treason. The party that had presided over the most serious intelligence failure in American history presented itself as the guardian of national security. The party that had turned a budget surplus into the largest deficit in history presented itself as the party of fiscal responsibility. The party that had launched a war on false pretences presented itself as the champion of truth.

But it would be a mistake to conclude that it was all double-speak. The most ominous statements at the convention were not double-speak at all. They meant exactly what they said. When Brownback spoke of a culture war, he meant a culture war. When the closing prayer invoked the forces of evil, it meant the forces of evil. When Miller called Kerry weak and wobbly, he meant that Kerry and everything he represents must be defeated and destroyed. The convention's modus operandi was to wrap extremism in the language of moderation — but the extremism was real, and it was not accidental.

The Republican party that gathered in New York in 2004 was not the party of Lincoln, or of Roosevelt, or of Eisenhower. It was a party that had been captured by an activist base driven by religious conviction and military fervour, sustained by a moderate electorate that does not fully understand what it has signed up for. Whether the moderates will eventually wake up is one of the great unanswered questions of American politics. But the activists are not waiting. They know what they want. And they are getting it.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_34
body:
There have been three great left-wing electoral landslides in Britain since the Victorian era. The first was New Labour's triumph in 1997, repeated in diminished but still extraordinary form in 2001, and it belongs to Tony Blair. The second was Labour's transformative victory of 1945, and it belongs to Clement Attlee. The third was the Liberal landslide of 1906, and it belongs to a man whom almost no one now remembers. His name was Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman, known to everyone in his day as CB, and it is roughly a hundred years since he took office and launched the longest continuous period of left-of-centre government in British history.

That period lasted nearly 17 years, from CB's election victory in January 1906 to the fall of the Lloyd George coalition in October 1922. It remains unmatched. No other government of the left in this country has held power for anything like as long. The era is naturally associated with its more flamboyant and more exhaustively chronicled figures — with Asquith, who succeeded CB in 1908, and above all with Lloyd George, who eventually supplanted Asquith and dominated the age. But the pivotal victory of 1906, the landslide that made everything else possible, belonged to CB alone. He won it. He set the tone. And then, before he could fully reap what he had sown, he died.

It is worth pausing over what kind of man he was, because the personality is part of the case. Margot Asquith, who was not given to dispensing compliments carelessly, called him "essentially a bon vivant, a boulevardier and a humorist." You have to go back to Disraeli to find a prime minister of whom such things could plausibly be said, and you will struggle to find one since. CB's biographer John Wilson described him as easy-going, lacking in ambition, and inclined to let well alone. He kept politics in proportion. He was no workaholic. He bore no grudges. In a profession that rewards obsessives and punishes the relaxed, CB went through life with the temperament of a man who knew that there were things that mattered more than the next vote in the Commons.

Do not mistake this, though, for laziness or empty pragmatism. CB was not an intellectual. He would not have pretended to be one. But he possessed deep and unwavering Liberal convictions, comparable in their quiet constancy to his lifelong love of Balzac and Flaubert, whom he read in the original French and whom he regarded as permanent companions rather than as ornaments of cultivation. In 1898, two years before he became leader of the Liberal Party, he made a speech defining Liberalism in terms that still read well over a century later. Liberalism meant, he said, self-government, trust in the general sense of mankind, opposition to artificial privilege, and laws that do not aggravate inequality but instead secure to every person the chance of a good and useful life. You could print that on a membership card today and no one would feel short-changed.

Three things in particular make CB worth admiring. The first is his stand on the Boer War. The Liberal Party nearly tore itself apart over that conflict, with much of the party hierarchy under Lord Rosebery supporting the war and the radicals opposing it with varying degrees of fury. As the new leader, CB initially sought a middle course, as leaders do. But he was not a man who could sustain moral equivocation indefinitely, and in 1901 he delivered what was arguably the most important anti-war speech of the era, condemning the Salisbury government's use of concentration camps in South Africa as "methods of barbarism." The phrase detonated. It is still detonating. It cost him friends and allies within his own party, but it established him as a man who would not trim on a question of principle when the principle was large enough.

The second is his pragmatic radicalism toward the emerging Labour Party. CB liked Labour figures. He sympathised with their grievances. He did not regard them as enemies to be crushed or as naive upstarts to be patronised. Within months of taking office, he delivered the 1906 Trades Disputes Act, which gave trade unions legal immunity from damages claims arising from strike action. It is no exaggeration to say that this single piece of legislation became the cornerstone of 20th-century labour law in Britain. What is less widely known is that CB had to fight off efforts by Asquith, then his chancellor, to weaken the bill. He pushed a stronger version through cabinet and got it onto the statute book. The Labour movement has had few better friends in Downing Street.

The third is his sheer political skill. He led from the centre, rebuilt the party from a wounding defeat in 1900 to a massive win in 1906, overturning a Tory majority of 134 and converting it into a Liberal majority of 130. That is one of the largest swings in British electoral history. He did it by exploiting his opponents' weaknesses — above all, their disastrous embrace of Joseph Chamberlain's protectionist tariff campaign — without overpromising. Most of his colleagues thought his strategy was wrong. They wanted a clearer, more aggressive pitch. CB held his nerve, kept his counsel, and the result vindicated him completely. It is worth reflecting on how rare that combination of patience and timing is in politics, and how often the people who lack it are the ones we remember instead.

His government's performance in office outperformed expectations, which is itself a tribute to the quality of the team he assembled. Had the Tory-dominated House of Lords not blocked legislation with such reckless abandon, CB would have achieved education reform, the abolition of the multiple franchise, and trade union law reform within his first year. Even so, ministers like Richard Haldane, who reorganised the army, and Lloyd George, who pushed through merchant shipping reform, proved themselves genuine reformers of the first rank. CB's achievement was to create the conditions in which such men could flourish.

Enoch Powell once observed that all political careers end in failure. He was writing about Joseph Chamberlain, and the dictum has a melancholy plausiveness that has made it stick. But it does not apply to CB, except in the most literal and unavoidable sense. He died in Downing Street in the early weeks of 1908, having resigned the premiership on his deathbed in favour of Asquith. He went out at the top. There was no decline, no humiliation, no slow fade into irrelevance. He was prime minister, and then he was gone.

Which brings us, finally, to the question of oblivion. Andrew Bonar Law is traditionally cited as the supreme example of prime ministerial obscurity, rescued from the shadows by Robert Blake's fine biography, appropriately titled "The Unknown Prime Minister." But Bonar Law's claim to that title is weaker than it once was. Alec Douglas-Home may yet recede further into the darkness as the years pass and the memory of the 1960s dims. For now, though, the title of Britain's unknown prime minister unjustly belongs to the admirable and neglected Henry Campbell-Bannerman, the man who won the greatest Liberal victory of them all and launched the longest era of left-of-centre government this country has ever known. He deserves better. He deserves, at the very least, to be remembered.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_37
body:
On the morning of the 1983 general election, I found myself in a committee room in Hackney, talking to the local Labour ward secretary about what the day would bring. I told him, as cheerfully as I could manage, that the Tories were going to win by more than 100 seats. He looked at me with the kind of pity that only a true believer can summon for a lost soul, and said that Labour would win by 20. I was right, of course, though the reality was even worse than I had suggested. Thatcher returned with a majority of 144. The ward secretary was not right about anything. Fourteen years later, on election day 1997, I asked the Labour leader what he thought the result would be. Maybe a win by 50, he said, with the cautious air of a man who has learned not to tempt fate. More like 150, I replied. As it turned out, Labour won by 179. In both cases, my secret was not insider knowledge, still less any special political insight. It was simply that I believed what the opinion polls said. The polls said Thatcher was heading for a landslide in 1983, and they said Blair was heading for one in 1997. I believed them. I was right.

I relate these stories not to boast — there is nothing to boast about in believing what the evidence tells you — but because the same question is now being put to me, in a different context, with the same mixture of hope and incredulity. When I am asked about the upcoming US presidential election on November 2, 2004, I say that John Kerry is winning. This is not, as some of my more hostile right-wing American email correspondents insist, because I am a closet Democrat, a European pinko, a Bush-hater or, as one memorable correspondent put it last week, a "limey jerk-off." It is because that is what the polls currently show. Kerry is ahead, narrowly but consistently, especially in the battleground states where this election, like all recent American presidential elections, will actually be decided. The polls say Kerry is winning. I believe them. But here an important caveat must be entered. Saying that Kerry is ahead in the polls is not the same thing as predicting that Kerry will win. Polls are snapshots, not predictions. They have margins of error. The election is still months away. Things will change. All one can say, on the basis of the current evidence, is that it is close and that Kerry is narrowly ahead. That may not satisfy those who want certainties. But it is what the evidence supports.

If poll-believers like me have some explaining to do, though, it is not only because of American electoral uncertainty. It is also because of an apparently glaring contradiction closer to home. Two recent British polls have produced results that, at first sight, seem to cancel each other out. A Guardian-ICM poll found Iraq at the bottom of a list of prospective election issues, with only 12% of respondents selecting it as a factor in deciding how they would vote. Yet an FT-Mori poll, published around the same time, found that defence and foreign affairs were the most important issues facing the country. Respected observers, including Peter Preston, have publicly expressed confusion at this disparity. If people care so much about defence and foreign affairs, how can Iraq be so low on their list of election priorities? If Iraq is so unimportant electorally, how can foreign affairs top the national agenda? The answer, I would argue, is that the confusion is more apparent than real. And it matters, for reasons of civic good as much as anything else, that we stamp out the lazy conceits that polls cannot be trusted or that they can be made to say anything. The truth is more straightforward and more interesting than that.

The resolution lies in the questions. Different polls ask different questions, and the answers they elicit reflect those differences. ICM asked respondents to pick from a prompted list which issues would be most important in deciding how they would vote. Presented with a menu of options, just 12% chose Iraq, while the NHS topped the list at 59%. Mori, by contrast, asked an unprompted question about the most important issue facing Britain today, without offering any list at all. In response, defence, foreign affairs and international terrorism came top at 24%, rising to 38% when respondents' mentions of other important issues were included. The two polls are not contradictory. They are compatible. It is perfectly possible — indeed, it is the most natural thing in the world — to see a subject as the most important national issue while not weighting it heavily in your voting decision. People do this all the time, and it is not a sign of stupidity or bad faith. It is a sign that they distinguish between what worries them about the world and what determines their vote.

There is a clear historical parallel here. In the 1980s, polls regularly showed that unemployment was the most important issue facing the country. They also showed that Labour had the best policies on it. Yet people voted Conservative, election after election, because on the voting-day issues — taxes, prices, economic competence, the feel of the leadership — the Tories had the advantage. The Tories did not win because people were unconcerned about unemployment. They won because, when it came to the actual business of choosing a government, other considerations weighed more heavily. A similar dynamic is at work today. Foreign affairs and terrorism may be the most important national issue, and people may be genuinely anxious about them. But when it comes to elections, people vote on health, education, personal economic security and the everyday things that touch their lives most directly. This is why Labour will win the next election — just as the polls say they will. The polls are not saying that people do not care about Iraq. They are saying that Iraq, for all its gravity, is not the thing that will determine how most people vote. And that is a distinction which, far from exposing the unreliability of polling, actually confirms its subtlety and its value.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_39
body:
It is one of the hardest things in public life to speak honestly about a cause you hold dear. The true believer, by definition, has committed something irretrievable to the object of his devotion. To criticise it is not merely to pass judgment on a policy or an institution; it is to interrogate oneself, to risk dismantling the very framework through which one understands the world. Conscience and loyalty are thus perpetually at war, and the conflict between them never produces an easy winner. The loyalist who suppresses his doubts may preserve the appearance of solidarity, but at the cost of his own integrity. The dissenter who breaks ranks may salve his conscience, but at the cost of the influence that loyalty confers. Most politicians, not surprisingly, prefer the discomfort of compromise to the clarity of rupture.

Tony Blair's deep, almost instinctive belief in the importance of the American alliance is a classic case of the perils of undifferentiated loyalty. Few modern British prime ministers have invested so much of themselves in a single international relationship. Few have been so willing to subordinate other considerations, domestic and European alike, to the preservation of that bond. And few have found themselves so thoroughly trapped by the consequences. Blair's current difficulties over Iraq are not, as his more unreflective critics maintain, simply the product of deceit or naivety. They are the product of a genuine struggle between enduring instincts and threatening realities, between the world as Blair needs it to be and the world as it actually is.

Begin with a proposition that Blair has never quite stated publicly but which has governed his political imagination throughout his career: that British domestic politics operate, in a real sense, downstream from America. Blair looked at Bill Clinton's election in 1992 and saw something more than a Democratic victory after 12 years of Republican rule. He saw a demonstration that a centrist, modernising, progressively inclined party could win again in an advanced democracy, provided it understood the temper of the times. Clinton's re-election in 1996 confirmed the lesson. By the time Blair won his own landslide in May 1997, he had absorbed the Clinton lesson so thoroughly that Labour's victory seemed not merely parallel to the American experience but almost consequent upon it. A Democratic White House made Labour appear aligned with the grain of history. It gave the project credibility. It made the future seem to point in a direction that Blair could inhabit with confidence.

George W. Bush's election in 2000 posed a serious challenge to this mental architecture. Here was a president who seemed to embody everything Blair's modernising project was defined against: social conservatism, unilateralist instincts, a suspicion of the very multilateralism that Clinton and Blair had made their shared signature. Blair's response was characteristic. He rushed to be the first foreign visitor to Camp David in early 2001, determined to establish a personal rapport with Bush and, just as importantly, determined to prevent the Conservative opposition from claiming to be the party in touch with the new America. William Hague, and later Iain Duncan Smith, might have positioned themselves as the natural interlocutors of a Republican White House. Blair was not going to give them the chance. The speed of his courtship was not merely diplomatic courtesy. It was a preemptive strike in domestic politics, a determination to control the narrative regardless of who occupied the Oval Office.

From this domestic calculation, Blair's belief widened into something more ambitious. He developed, or at any rate articulated, a doctrine: that the United States is the indispensable nation for settling global problems, and that the Anglo-American relationship is the catalyst that keeps America engaged in the world rather than retreating into isolationism. This was not an ignoble doctrine. It rested on a plausible reading of the 20th century, in which American power, however imperfectly exercised, had been the decisive factor in defeating fascism and containing communism. But there was always an ambiguity at its heart. How much did Blair truly believe in the "special relationship" mystique, with its evocations of shared sacrifice and common destiny? And how much was he, by temperament and conviction, a European who saw the American bond as instrumental rather than sacred? The two identities were not necessarily incompatible, but they were not identical either.

The ambiguity was on vivid display at a White House dinner in 1998, when Blair, seated alongside Clinton, quoted the words that Harry Hopkins had spoken to Churchill in 1941: "Whither thou goest I will go... Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God. Even to the end." It was a remarkable thing for a British prime minister to say, not least because it cast Britain in the role of Ruth and America in the role of Naomi, the younger partner pledging devotion to the elder. But it left no doubt about where Blair's heart lay. Whatever his European credentials, whatever his commitment to integrating Britain into the continent, the American alliance was the relationship to which all others were, in the final analysis, subordinate.

The difficulty, of course, is that the America to which Blair pledged this devotion was Clinton's America. Bush's America is a fundamentally different proposition. It is true that Blair sometimes struggled to secure American engagement even under Clinton, as the agonising over Kosovo demonstrated. But those difficulties occurred within a shared framework of assumptions about multilateralism, international institutions and the legitimacy of collective action. Bush presents a problem of a different order altogether. As Frances Fitzgerald observed in the New York Review of Books, the Bush administration broke with the internationalist premises accepted by every postwar administration except, briefly, Reagan's first. This was not a shift of emphasis. It was a rupture with the tradition that Blair had always assumed was permanent.

Part of the problem is that Bush has rarely defined his foreign-policy goals in concrete terms. He speaks vaguely of threats and of US-defined security solutions, but rarely of the kind of measurable, collective objectives that might anchor a shared allied strategy. During the 2000 campaign, Bush remarked with characteristic bluntness: "We're not so sure who the they are, but we know they're there." It was a revealing formulation. It licensed a posture of permanent vigilance against an undefined enemy, and it did so in a language that made allied consultation seem almost beside the point. If you do not know who "they" are, why would you need allies to help you deal with them?

The problem deepens when one examines the agenda set by Bush's subordinates. Dick Cheney, Donald Rumsfeld, and especially John Bolton have set a course that repudiates collective action and alliances as matters of principle, not merely of expedience. Bolton's claim that "there is no such thing as the United Nations" and that the international community is merely what the United States can lead when it suits its interests was not an offhand remark. It was a statement of doctrine. So too was the Pentagon's shift, under Rumsfeld, from a "threat-based strategy" to a "capabilities-based approach." This seemingly technical change allowed the US military to request vast increases in spending without having to justify them against any identifiable enemy. Rumsfeld spoke of defending "against the unknown," a formulation that converted military planning from a response to danger into a permanent condition of readiness against phantoms. It is the language of a power that does not see itself as part of an international order, but as an order unto itself.

This reality constantly undermines Blair's efforts to portray the Bush administration as a willing partner in the moral internationalist order he championed in his Brighton conference speech and reiterated to the TUC. Blair speaks of the need for collective action, of the obligations of the powerful, of the unity of the international community. Bush's Washington speaks a different language altogether, and the gap between the two is not closing. One can feel a certain sympathy for Blair here. He believes in the right things. He is trying to exert necessary influence on an administration that is, by instinct and ideology, resistant to the kind of influence he wishes to exert. He is, in his own way, fighting a lonely and not entirely dishonourable battle to keep America engaged with the world on terms that the world can accept.

But sympathy has its limits. Blair earns grief at home and diminishing respect in Washington because of a profound disjunction between what he wants to believe about the administration and what is actually true. He wants to believe that Bush shares his vision of a world ordered by law, collective security and shared values. The evidence that Bush does not share this vision is by now overwhelming. The administration has discarded the rules by which Blair wants to play. Rather than confronting that reality, Blair pretends it does not exist. This is the classic error of the over-loyalist, who persuades himself that fidelity to the alliance matters more than clarity about the ally. In the end, loyalty that overrides truth is not loyalty at all. It is self-deception, and it serves neither the cause nor the man.
